EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread westerly wind gusts of 45 to 60 mph occurred from late morning into the early evening across southern WI. Isolated tree damage occurred across the area. Strong low pressure tracked across southeast WI to northern Lake MI and into Canada. As the low moved away from southern WI, the strong westerly wind gusts began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Westerly wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph resulted in isolated tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow melt and some rainfall led to minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fox River near New Munster reached minor flood stage, cresting at 11.66 feet the early afternoon of February 7th. Water surrounds a home near the intersection of Highway 50 and Highway W in the Town of Wheatland area. Water is in the back yards of some homes in the Village of Salem Lakes and Town of Wheatland areas. Floodwaters are about 6 inches deep over 77th Street in the Town of Wheatland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and a strong frontal boundary brought moderate to heavy snow to southern WI. The winter storm started with areas of freezing drizzle and light snow before the heavier snow began. Many vehicle slide-off and accidents occurred. Some schools and other events were cancelled on January 23rd. A man collapsed and died while shoveling in Milwaukee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow accumulation and areas of freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure area brought a prolonged period of light to moderate snowfall. Lake enhanced snow contributed to the overall totals for the counties bordering Lake MI. Two deaths occurred when a semi-truck plunged into Mirror Lake after sliding off the I-90/94 bridge near Lake Delton. Other vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred across southern WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to five inches of snow accumulation with the highest amounts over the southern portion of the county. Two deaths occurred when a semi-truck plunged into Mirror Lake after sliding off the I-90/94 bridge near Lake Delton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moving across the Ohio River Valley spread a swath of moderate to heavy snow across southern WI. Lake effect snow contributed to the heavy snow accumulations near Lake MI. Many vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred especially in the Milwaukee metro area. Two people were killed in a car accident in Sheboygan County. A few schools closed early on January 18th over far southern and southwest WI. There were 2 indirect deaths after shoveling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was day 3 of a prolonged cold period across the CWA. A weak system had passed through the area during the day on the 26th with a reinforcing shot of cold air arriving behind it. Skies cleared out by early evening with temperatures dropping into the 20s and 30s below zero over a wide area. Winds were able to remain light through the night leading to wind chills of 40 to 50 below zero in some areas.

Cold air moved into northern Wisconsin as a strong low pressure system departed and high pressure over the Plains moved toward the region. Low temperatures on the morning of January 30th were mainly in the -15��F to -30��F range and wind chills mostly -35 to -55. Temperatures across the area remained below zero throughout the day on the 30th. Schools and many businesses closed, public transportation suspended or curtailed service, and the U.S. Postal Service even suspended deliveries due to the bitter cold.||Several locations set or tied their records for the coldest daytime high temperatures on January 30th including: Appleton (Outagamie Co.); Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.); Marshfield (Wood Co.); Merrill (Lincoln Co.); Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.); Wausau (Marathon Co.); and Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.).||Record low temperatures were tied or broken on the morning of the 31st at Appleton, Green Bay (Brown Co.), Marshfield, and Oshkosh.||Several locations set or tied their records for the coldest daytime high temperatures on January 31st including: Antigo (Langlade Co.); Appleton; Manitowoc; Marshfield; Oshkosh; Stevens Point (Portage Co.); Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.); and Wisconsin Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low temperature in Antigo was -28 degrees on the morning of January 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm covered western Wisconsin with a fresh blanket of snow in late January. The snow fell from the evening of January 27th through much of the day on the 28th. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 12 inches were common and the highest reported totals were 12 inches in Camp Douglas (Juneau County) and 11.5 inches near Tomah (Monroe County). The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency because of the snow. Most western Wisconsin schools were closed January 28th following the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 6 and 9 inches of snow fell across Adams County. The highest reported total was 9.6 inches in Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With arctic air in place over western Wisconsin, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero on the morning of the 30th with several sites falling into the 30s and 40s below zero on the morning of the 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 57 below zero in Medford (Taylor County) during the morning of January 30th. One person suffered hypothermia related injuries near Hazel Green (Grant County) when they were found outdoors after about an hour. A power outage occurred across northern Grant County during the morning of January 30th that affected approximately 360 households. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency because of the cold weather. Most western Wisconsin schools were closed from January 28th through the 31st. La Crosse (La Crosse County) city libraries closed because of the cold and this had never occurred in the past 34 years. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of western Wisconsin on January 30th and 31st. Classes at UW-La Crosse were canceled on January 30th and 31st while both Viterbo and Western Technical College canceled classes on the 30th and the morning of the 31st. More than 1,000 elderly people across La Crosse County did not receive meals during the cold outbreak as the county's senior nutrition program was closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 55 below zero were common across Trempealeau County from the evening of January 29th into the morning of the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and snow occurred across portions of western Wisconsin on January 22nd and 23rd as a winter storm moved across the Midwest. The storm produced several rounds of precipitation which started out as freezing drizzle during the morning of the 22nd that coated most surfaces with a light glaze. Snow then occurred with accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. The highest reported totals were 10.5 inches in Potosi (Grant County) and 9 inches near Burton (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 4 and 6 inches of snow fell across Adams County. The highest reported total was 3.8 inches in Friendship. The snow was preceded by a period of freezing drizzle that produced a light glaze of ice accumulation.

Snow then occurred with accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. The highest reported totals were 10.5 inches in Potosi (Grant County) and 9 inches near Burton (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 3 and 4 inches of snow fell across Crawford County. The highest reported total was 4 inches near Mount Zion. The snow was preceded by a period of freezing drizzle that produced a light glaze of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and snow occurred across portions of western Wisconsin on January 22nd and 23rd as a winter storm moved across the Midwest. The storm produced several rounds of precipitation which started out as freezing drizzle during the morning of the 22nd that coated most surfaces with a light glaze. Snow then occurred with accumulations of 2 to 10 inches. The highest reported totals were 10.5 inches in Potosi (Grant County) and 9 inches near Burton (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 10 inches of snow fell across Grant County. The highest reported total was 10.5 inches in Potosi. The snow was preceded by a period of freezing drizzle that produced a light glaze of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With arctic air in place over western Wisconsin, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero on the morning of the 30th with several sites falling into the 30s and 40s below zero on the morning of the 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 57 below zero in Medford (Taylor County) during the morning of January 30th. One person suffered hypothermia related injuries near Hazel Green (Grant County) when they were found outdoors after about an hour. A power outage occurred across northern Grant County during the morning of January 30th that affected approximately 360 households. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency because of the cold weather. Most western Wisconsin schools were closed from January 28th through the 31st. La Crosse (La Crosse County) city libraries closed because of the cold and this had never occurred in the past 34 years. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of western Wisconsin on January 30th and 31st. Classes at UW-La Crosse were canceled on January 30th and 31st while both Viterbo and Western Technical College canceled classes on the 30th and the morning of the 31st. More than 1,000 elderly people across La Crosse County did not receive meals during the cold outbreak as the county's senior nutrition program was closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 55 below zero were common across La Crosse County from the evening of January 29th into the morning of the 31st. The coldest wind chill to occur was 52 below zero at a mesonet station near St. Joseph. La Crosse city libraries closed due to the cold and this had never occurred in the past 34 years. Classes at UW-La Crosse were canceled on January 30th and 31st while both Viterbo and Western Technical College canceled classes on the 30th and the morning of the 31st. More than 1,000 elderly people across La Crosse County did not receive meals during the cold outbreak as the county's senior nutrition program was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a surface boundary to produce several hours of moderate to heavy snow across northeast Wisconsin. Six to eight inches of snow fell in less than 10 hours. The highest measured storm total snowfall was 7.5 inches in Florence (Florence Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell in Forest County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that passed southeast of Wisconsin and an upper level disturbance that moved across the state produced heavy snow and gusty winds across much of the area. The winds were strong enough to cause blowing and drifting of the newly fallen snow. Snowfall totals from the storm were in excess of a foot at several locations in eastern Wisconsin including 14.5 inches in Valders (Manitowoc Co.), 14.0  inches in Forestville (Door Co.), and 12.0 inches in Luxemburg (Kewaunee Co.). Some of the higher totals across the rest of the area included 12.6 inches in Denmark (Brown Co.), 10.5 inches in Shiocton (Outagamie Co.), 10.2 inches in Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.), and 10.0 inches in Brillion (Calumet Co.).||Snowfall records for January 28th were tied or broken in Marshfield (Wood Co.), Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.), Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Green Bay (Brown Co.), Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.), Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.), and Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Brown County included 12.6 inches in Denmark, 11.5 inches near De Pere, 11.0 inches on the east side of Green Bay, 10.2 inches in Ledgeview, and 9.7 inches at the National Weather Service in Green Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air moved into northern Wisconsin as a strong low pressure system departed and high pressure over the Plains moved toward the region. Low temperatures on the morning of January 30th were mainly in the -15��F to -30��F range and wind chills mostly -35 to -55. Temperatures across the area remained below zero throughout the day on the 30th. Schools and many businesses closed, public transportation suspended or curtailed service, and the U.S. Postal Service even suspended deliveries due to the bitter cold.||Several locations set or tied their records for the coldest daytime high temperatures on January 30th including: Appleton (Outagamie Co.); Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.); Marshfield (Wood Co.); Merrill (Lincoln Co.); Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.); Wausau (Marathon Co.); and Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.).||Record low temperatures were tied or broken on the morning of the 31st at Appleton, Green Bay (Brown Co.), Marshfield, and Oshkosh.||Several locations set or tied their records for the coldest daytime high temperatures on January 31st including: Antigo (Langlade Co.); Appleton; Manitowoc; Marshfield; Oshkosh; Stevens Point (Portage Co.); Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.); and Wisconsin Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low temperature near Forestville was -22 degrees on the morning of January 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of lake-effect snow showers developed over Lake Michigan and then moved onshore during the evening of January 18th. This resulted in localized heavy snow near Two Rivers (Manitowoc Co.). More than a foot of new snow was measured in Two Rivers after the snow ended early on the afternoon of January 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 12.6 inches of snow fell in Two Rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavier snow over eastern Wisconsin developed within a larger area of snow associated with a low pressure system that passed southeast of Wisconsin. The snow band produced 6 to 9 inches of accumulation, most of which fell in less than 12 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Brown County included 9.0 inches in Suring, 8.5 inches in Hobart, and 7.5 inches in Green Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heading into the last few days of January, an historically cold air mass settled over the region. This led to a prolonged period a sub-zero temperatures and dangerously cold wind chills. Many schools were closed for 2-3 days due to the cold. The coldest temperatures were seen January 30th into the 31st. All 5 of our climate sites recorded record low high temperatures on the 30th. Wind chills during this stretch remained in the 20s and 30s below zero with numerous locations reaching into the 40s below zero during the early morning hours. Temperatures finally started warming on the 31st as the core of the high moved to the east and warmer air began to move in ahead of an area of low pressure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heading into the last few days of January, an historically cold air mass settled over the region. This led to a prolonged period a sub-zero temperatures and dangerously cold wind chills. Many schools were closed for 2-3 days due to the cold. The coldest temperatures were seen January 30th into the 31st. All 5 of our climate sites recorded record low high temperatures on the 30th. Wind chills during this stretch remained in the 20s and 30s below zero with numerous locations reaching into the 40s below zero during the early morning hours. Temperatures finally started warming on the 31st as the core of the high moved to the east and warmer air began to move in ahead of an area of low pressure.

With arctic air in place over western Wisconsin, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero on the morning of the 30th with several sites falling into the 30s and 40s below zero on the morning of the 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 57 below zero in Medford (Taylor County) during the morning of January 30th. One person suffered hypothermia related injuries near Hazel Green (Grant County) when they were found outdoors after about an hour. A power outage occurred across northern Grant County during the morning of January 30th that affected approximately 360 households. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency because of the cold weather. Most western Wisconsin schools were closed from January 28th through the 31st. La Crosse (La Crosse County) city libraries closed because of the cold and this had never occurred in the past 34 years. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of western Wisconsin on January 30th and 31st. Classes at UW-La Crosse were canceled on January 30th and 31st while both Viterbo and Western Technical College canceled classes on the 30th and the morning of the 31st. More than 1,000 elderly people across La Crosse County did not receive meals during the cold outbreak as the county's senior nutrition program was closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 55 below zero were common across Buffalo County from the afternoon of January 29th into the morning of the 31st. The coldest wind chill to occur was 52 below zero at a mesonet station in Cochrane.

Initially, the morning of Sunday, January 27th, a ridge of high pressure was centered across the Upper Midwest. This ridge moved southeast with an area of low pressure developing over Alberta, Canada, and diving southeast across the Dakotas Sunday afternoon, and over northern Iowa, and northern Illinois through Sunday night/Monday morning.||This was a typical Alberta Clipper which raced southeast, with an initial very dry air mass over the Upper Midwest. Thus, moisture content was low, the speed was high, and the atmosphere had to moisten up before snow reached the surface. In addition, the forcing weakened during the afternoon, and evening across southern Minnesota, before intensifying across Wisconsin Sunday night. ||These factors limited snowfall amounts with narrow bands of 6 to 8 inches of snow across southern Minnesota, and portions of west central Wisconsin. The heavier amounts, or greater than 8 inches, occurred in southeast Minnesota, and into southern Wisconsin. ||The following are some of the heavier snowfall amounts:||7.5 inches in Eau Claire.|7.0 inches in Menomonie.|6.4 inches north of Eau Claire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in Chippewa were based on the official airport, and media observer of 6.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a weekend storm system that brought measurable snowfall to all of central and southern Minnesota, and the adjacent areas of west central Wisconsin, a surge of Arctic air moved southward.||The core of the coldest air mass (that hadn't been felt across the Upper Midwest since the Winter of 1995-96) moved southward from Monday afternoon, January 28th, through Wednesday evening, January 30th. The center of the cold air mass moved across Minnesota the afternoon of Tuesday, January 29th where 850mb temperatures were around -38F. Although temperatures slowly modified above the surface past the afternoon of Tuesday, January 29th, the combination of very strong winds and temperatures in the teens and 20s below zero, wind chill values averaged between 45 and 59 degrees below zero from Tuesday afternoon, through Wednesday morning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several local weather sources measured wind chill values lower than -35F from Tuesday afternoon, through Thursday morning. The coldest wind chill was -57F at the Cumberland airport.

Although wind speeds lowered on Wednesday, actual temperatures in the 20s and 30s below zero kept wind chill values below -35F through Thursday morning. ||The coldest measured wind chill of -59F in west central Wisconsin was at the Menomonie airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several local weather sources measured wind chill values lower than -35F from Tuesday afternoon, through Thursday morning. The coldest wind chill was -53F at the Eau Claire airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With arctic air in place over western Wisconsin, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero on the morning of the 30th with several sites falling into the 30s and 40s below zero on the morning of the 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 57 below zero in Medford (Taylor County) during the morning of January 30th. One person suffered hypothermia related injuries near Hazel Green (Grant County) when they were found outdoors after about an hour. A power outage occurred across northern Grant County during the morning of January 30th that affected approximately 360 households. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency because of the cold weather. Most western Wisconsin schools were closed from January 28th through the 31st. La Crosse (La Crosse County) city libraries closed because of the cold and this had never occurred in the past 34 years. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of western Wisconsin on January 30th and 31st. Classes at UW-La Crosse were canceled on January 30th and 31st while both Viterbo and Western Technical College canceled classes on the 30th and the morning of the 31st. More than 1,000 elderly people across La Crosse County did not receive meals during the cold outbreak as the county's senior nutrition program was closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 55 below zero were common across Jackson County from the evening of January 29th into the morning of the 31st. The coldest wind chill to occur was 54 below zero at the automated weather observing equipment for the Black River Falls airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that passed southeast of Wisconsin and an upper level disturbance that moved across the state produced heavy snow and gusty winds across much of the area. The winds were strong enough to cause blowing and drifting of the newly fallen snow. Snowfall totals from the storm were in excess of a foot at several locations in eastern Wisconsin including 14.5 inches in Valders (Manitowoc Co.), 14.0  inches in Forestville (Door Co.), and 12.0 inches in Luxemburg (Kewaunee Co.). Some of the higher totals across the rest of the area included 12.6 inches in Denmark (Brown Co.), 10.5 inches in Shiocton (Outagamie Co.), 10.2 inches in Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.), and 10.0 inches in Brillion (Calumet Co.).||Snowfall records for January 28th were tied or broken in Marshfield (Wood Co.), Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.), Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Green Bay (Brown Co.), Oshkosh (Winnebago Co.), Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.), and Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Wood County included 8.0 inches in Wisconsin Rapids and Saratoga, and 6.0 inches near Marshfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from southern Minnesota into northern Wisconsin the 6th into the 7th. This track set up an inverted trough and deformation zone across much of the forecast area with widespread heavy snowfall reported. Multiple waves of heavy snowfall occurred along with areas of freezing drizzle in between waves. Most locations saw between 8 and 12 of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 was reported just southwest of Phillips.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Texas Panhandle to the Ohio Valley on the 11th into the 12th. An inverted trough then set up off the low and passed through northeastern Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin. An area of enhanced lift moved through north-central Wisconsin and contributed to higher snowfall amounts in the Price, Ashland and Iron County area. Snowfall totals in northwest Wisconsin ranged from 5 to 15 with numerous reports of 14 to 15 near Phillips and 14 in Gile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving low pressure system spread snow across much of the Northland during the morning hours. Snowfall became heavy during the afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour and whiteout conditions. A deep low pressure system moved across southern Wisconsin spreading snow across the Northland. The heaviest snow fell across northwestern Wisconsin beginning late in the afternoon of the 23rd and continuing overnight. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches from southern Sawyer County to the Bayfield Peninsula. Some of the highest reports included 12 south of Phillips, 9.5 near Lorretta, 11 in Gile and 6 near Herbster. Strong north winds were seen behind the storm with sustained winds at 25 to 30 mph gusting to around 40 mph, creating dangerous blizzard-like conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of historically cold arctic air settled over southern WI. Windy conditions and low temperatures in the -20s to -30s Fahrenheit resulted in wind chill temperatures of 35 below to 55 below zero for much of this period. High temperatures did not warm above 10 below zero for most locations on January 30th. Widespread government, school, and business closings were common on January 30-31st. The United States Postal Service suspended mail delivery on January 29-30th. Many water main breaks occurred especially in Madison and Milwaukee. Power outages occurred over portions of Milwaukee, Waukesha, Racine, and Kenosha Counties as power lines constricted and snapped in the extreme cold. A man was found frozen in his garage in Milwaukee and had collapsed after shoveling snow. Another man was found frozen in the snow in Cudahy and died from hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures of 35 below to 55 below zero.

A man was found frozen in the snow on January 31st. He was not dressed appropriately and died from hypothermia.

Temperatures were below zero Fahrenheit on January 31st into the early morning hours of February 1st. The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's Office determined the cause of death was hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fierce, late-winter storm produced heavy snow, freezing rain, and high winds across much of central and northern Wisconsin on February 23-24 as an area of low pressure rapidly deepened across the Great Lakes. At the height of the storm on the 24th, travel was not recommended across parts of central and northern Wisconsin as blizzard conditions made travel nearly impossible. The combination of ice, snow, and strong winds caused power outages and tree damage in many locations.||Strong winds and near-blizzard conditions shut down a few rural stretches of heavily traveled highways across east-central Wisconsin on February 24, including I-41 in Neenah (Winnebago Co.), where one motorist was killed and 71 others injured in a chain-reaction pileup involving 131 vehicles. This was the largest traffic accident in Wisconsin history.||The accident happened around 11:20 AM CST in the southbound lanes of I-41 at Highway G in Neenah. Cars, pickups, and semitrailers smashed together and sandwiched underneath each other. The interstate wasn't reopened until 3 AM on February 25. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals with the two-day storm included 18.9 inches near Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 16.1 inches at Rice Reservoir (Lincoln Co.), 15.9 inches near Argonne, 15.7 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 15.4 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), 12.7 near Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.), and 12.5 in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.). Parts of central and east-central Wisconsin received up to one-half inch of ice accumulation in addition to the snow.||Measured wind gusts in the 50 mph to 60 mph range were recorded at many locations, including: 60 mph in Green Bay (Brown Co.); 58 mph in Wausau (Marathon Co.); 57 mph in Kewaunee (Kewaunee Co.); 56 mph in Omro (Winnebago Co.); 53 mph near Zittau (Winnebago Co.), near Monico (Oneida Co.), in Marshfield (Wood Co.), and in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.); 52 mph in Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.) and in Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.); and 51 mph in Appleton (Outagamie Co.) and in Wautoma (Waushara Co.).||New daily snowfall records were set for February 24 in Rhinelander and Wausau. The 10.9 inches in Rhinelander broke the previous record of 5.8 inches that was set in 2017, and the 7.6 inches in Wausau broke the previous record of 7.3 inches that was set in 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew from the west. Sustained wind speeds were as high as 44 mph, with a gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding occurred along some western Wisconsin rivers during the middle of March. The flooding was prompted by runoff from rain earlier in the month along with melting snow and some ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain earlier in March along with melting snow pushed the Yellow River out of its banks in Necedah. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 16.61 feet on March 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fierce, late-winter storm produced heavy snow, freezing rain, and high winds across much of central and northern Wisconsin on February 23-24 as an area of low pressure rapidly deepened across the Great Lakes. At the height of the storm on the 24th, travel was not recommended across parts of central and northern Wisconsin as blizzard conditions made travel nearly impossible. The combination of ice, snow, and strong winds caused power outages and tree damage in many locations.||Strong winds and near-blizzard conditions shut down a few rural stretches of heavily traveled highways across east-central Wisconsin on February 24, including I-41 in Neenah (Winnebago Co.), where one motorist was killed and 71 others injured in a chain-reaction pileup involving 131 vehicles. This was the largest traffic accident in Wisconsin history.||The accident happened around 11:20 AM CST in the southbound lanes of I-41 at Highway G in Neenah. Cars, pickups, and semitrailers smashed together and sandwiched underneath each other. The interstate wasn't reopened until 3 AM on February 25. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals with the two-day storm included 18.9 inches near Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 16.1 inches at Rice Reservoir (Lincoln Co.), 15.9 inches near Argonne, 15.7 inches near Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 15.4 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), 12.7 near Boulder Junction (Vilas Co.), and 12.5 in Spread Eagle (Florence Co.). Parts of central and east-central Wisconsin received up to one-half inch of ice accumulation in addition to the snow.||Measured wind gusts in the 50 mph to 60 mph range were recorded at many locations, including: 60 mph in Green Bay (Brown Co.); 58 mph in Wausau (Marathon Co.); 57 mph in Kewaunee (Kewaunee Co.); 56 mph in Omro (Winnebago Co.); 53 mph near Zittau (Winnebago Co.), near Monico (Oneida Co.), in Marshfield (Wood Co.), and in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.); 52 mph in Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.) and in Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.); and 51 mph in Appleton (Outagamie Co.) and in Wautoma (Waushara Co.).||New daily snowfall records were set for February 24 in Rhinelander and Wausau. The 10.9 inches in Rhinelander broke the previous record of 5.8 inches that was set in 2017, and the 7.6 inches in Wausau broke the previous record of 7.3 inches that was set in 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow caused whiteout conditions in Oneida County for most of the afternoon. Gusts to 53 mph were recorded near Monico and at Rhinelander-Oneida County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of historically cold arctic air settled over southern WI. Windy conditions and low temperatures in the -20s to -30s Fahrenheit resulted in wind chill temperatures of 35 below to 55 below zero for much of this period. High temperatures did not warm above 10 below zero for most locations on January 30th. Widespread government, school, and business closings were common on January 30-31st. The United States Postal Service suspended mail delivery on January 29-30th. Many water main breaks occurred especially in Madison and Milwaukee. Power outages occurred over portions of Milwaukee, Waukesha, Racine, and Kenosha Counties as power lines constricted and snapped in the extreme cold. A man was found frozen in his garage in Milwaukee and had collapsed after shoveling snow. Another man was found frozen in the snow in Cudahy and died from hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures of 35 below to 55 below zero.

Parts of central and east-central Wisconsin received up to one-half inch of ice accumulation in addition to the snow.||Measured wind gusts in the 50 mph to 60 mph range were recorded at many locations, including: 60 mph in Green Bay (Brown Co.); 58 mph in Wausau (Marathon Co.); 57 mph in Kewaunee (Kewaunee Co.); 56 mph in Omro (Winnebago Co.); 53 mph near Zittau (Winnebago Co.), near Monico (Oneida Co.), in Marshfield (Wood Co.), and in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.); 52 mph in Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.) and in Manitowoc (Manitowoc Co.); and 51 mph in Appleton (Outagamie Co.) and in Wautoma (Waushara Co.).||New daily snowfall records were set for February 24 in Rhinelander and Wausau. The 10.9 inches in Rhinelander broke the previous record of 5.8 inches that was set in 2017, and the 7.6 inches in Wausau broke the previous record of 7.3 inches that was set in 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive chain-reaction pileup occurred in localized whiteout conditions along a stretch of I-41 in Neenah. A total of 131 vehicles were involved in the accident, 71 people were hospitalized and one person was killed after he exited his vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of historically cold arctic air settled over southern WI. Windy conditions and low temperatures in the -20s to -30s Fahrenheit resulted in wind chill temperatures of 35 below to 55 below zero for much of this period. High temperatures did not warm above 10 below zero for most locations on January 30th. Widespread government, school, and business closings were common on January 30-31st. The United States Postal Service suspended mail delivery on January 29-30th. Many water main breaks occurred especially in Madison and Milwaukee. Power outages occurred over portions of Milwaukee, Waukesha, Racine, and Kenosha Counties as power lines constricted and snapped in the extreme cold. A man was found frozen in his garage in Milwaukee and had collapsed after shoveling snow. Another man was found frozen in the snow in Cudahy and died from hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures of 35 below to 55 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Southern Plains on the 9th to northern lower Michigan on the 10th. This track brought an inverted trough through the Northland and resulted in snow that started on the evening of the 9th and continued into the 10th. The greatest snowfall amounts were in Bayfield and Sawyer Counties where 4 to 7 inches were recorded.

During the period from about March 10th through the 15th, daytime temperatures warmed well into the 40s with some locations reaching the 50s. These warm temperatures caused a rapid melt of the existing snowpack that averaged between 12 and 18 inches. The runoff from this snowmelt caused extensive flooding to occur across most of western Wisconsin. Families were displaced from homes, water went into basements and numerous roads were closed because of the flooding. Over 100 homes were affected by the flooding with almost 20 sustaining minor damage. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide state of emergency because of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow caused flooding to occur along the Buffalo River. State Highway 37 between State Highway 88 and County Highway NN south of Mondovi and several county and town roads were closed because of this flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several locations across northeast Wisconsin had snow depths around a foot or more above normal for late February and early March. Temperatures across central and northern Wisconsin warmed above freezing during the daytime hours starting around March 8th. Rain accompanied the warmer temperatures, and the runoff from the combination of rain and rapidly melting snow caused flooding of streams, rivers, ditches, roads and low-lying areas in central and east-central Wisconsin. Numerous roads had to be closed, around 50 homes sustained flood damage, and scores of people were displaced from their homes due to the flooding. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide state of emergency in Wisconsin due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall and snow melt combined with ice jams to cause flooding on rivers, streams and roads in the vicinity of Manitowoc and Two Rivers. Some residents living near the West Twin River had to be evacuated while county emergency crews worked to contain the overflowing Shoto Dam. The runoff also caused flooding of other low-lying and poor drainage areas. Two homes sustained major flood damage, and four homes and one business sustained minor damage. Some roads were also damaged by the flooding and required repairs after the water receded. The Manitowoc River reached major flood stage just west of Manitowoc, reaching a (preliminary) record stage of 13.58 feet. A county-wide state of emergency was declared on March 15th.

These warm temperatures caused a rapid melt of the existing snowpack that averaged between 12 and 18 inches. The runoff from this snowmelt caused extensive flooding to occur across most of western Wisconsin. Families were displaced from homes, water went into basements and numerous roads were closed because of the flooding. Over 100 homes were affected by the flooding with almost 20 sustaining minor damage. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide state of emergency because of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melting snow caused flooding to occur along most streams in Trempealeau County. One home collapsed in the Town of Arcadia from the flooding and numerous county and town roads were closed. State Highways 93 and 95 were reduced to one lane. In Dodge, 12 homes were evacuated due to ice jam flooding along the Trempealeau River. Around 30 homes and businesses sustained minor to major damage and another 45 were affected by the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow, along with ice jams, pushed the Trempealeau River out of its banks in Dodge. The river crested over four feet above the flood stage at 13.44 feet on March 16th. This was a crest of record for this site. The flooding prompted some residents to evacuate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall and snow melt combined to flood roads and low-lying areas, mainly in and around Park Ridge, Plover, Bancroft and Almond. The runoff also caused flooding of other low-lying and poor drainage areas. The Village of Plover's wastewater system was inundated with water and there was also considerable road and yard flooding in the village. Four homes sustained minor flood damage, and some roads were also damage by the flooding and required repairs after the water receded. A county-wide state of emergency was declared on March 14th and the City of Park Ridge also declared a state of emergency that same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were many factors that led to a severe Spring flood melt along the Eau Claire and Chippewa Rivers and several of their tributaries during the last two weeks of March. The first factor was above average snowpack for mid March. Additionally, the added effect of a cold late Winter kept this snowpack deeper into the early Spring. Once temperatures warmed, and snow began to melt, a few storms led to additional water on the already heavy snowpack. As area rivers, streams and creeks began to thaw, ice jams developed which exacerbated urban flooding. In addition, the Corp of Engineers closed some parks in west central Wisconsin due to the flood waters.||Based on the Wisconsin Emergency Management damage assessment, 20 homes were affected in Dunn County, with 4 receiving minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow began to melt, areal flooding started, especially along the smaller creeks and streams that lead into the Chippewa River. Two road closures occurred during the height of the flooding, and led to roadway erosion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several locations across northeast Wisconsin had snow depths around a foot or more above normal for late February and early March. Temperatures across central and northern Wisconsin warmed above freezing during the daytime hours starting around March 8th. Rain accompanied the warmer temperatures, and the runoff from the combination of rain and rapidly melting snow caused flooding of streams, rivers, ditches, roads and low-lying areas in central and east-central Wisconsin. Numerous roads had to be closed, around 50 homes sustained flood damage, and scores of people were displaced from their homes due to the flooding. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide state of emergency in Wisconsin due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall and snow melt combined with clogged storm drains and ice jams to cause flooding on rivers, streams, roads and low-lying areas throughout Brown County. The hardest hit area in Green Bay was along the East River, where first responders evacuated about 80 to 100 residents from their homes. Residents near Duck Creek in Howard were also evacuated due to flooding. The county EOC moved assets away from its site in Allouez on the evening of March 15th due to flooding in the vicinity. Some roads were damaged by the flooding and required repairs after flood waters receded. Twelve homes sustained major flood damage and twenty-four suffered minor damage. Pamperin Park in Howard remained closed for a month due to damage caused by the flooding. A county-wide state of emergency was declared on March 15th, and the City of Green Bay, Town of Ledgeview, and Village of Suamico also declared a states of emergency that same day. The Village of Bellevue declared a state of emergency on March 14th.

The Kewaunee River reached moderate flood stage just west of Kewaunee. A county-wide state of emergency was declared on March 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were many factors that led to a severe Spring flood melt along the Eau Claire and Chippewa Rivers and several of their tributaries during the last two weeks of March. The first factor was above average snowpack for mid March. Additionally, the added effect of a cold late Winter kept this snowpack deeper into the early Spring. Once temperatures warmed, and snow began to melt, a few storms led to additional water on the already heavy snowpack. As area rivers, streams and creeks began to thaw, ice jams developed which exacerbated urban flooding. In addition, the Corp of Engineers closed some parks in west central Wisconsin due to the flood waters.||Based on the Wisconsin Emergency Management damage assessment, 20 homes were affected in Dunn County, with 4 receiving minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As snow began to melt, areal flooding started, especially along the smaller creeks and streams that lead into the Chippewa River. There was a minor ice jam on the Chippewa River near Durand that was reported the afternoon of March 20 which caused the river level to rapidly rise in the downtown area. The water level spiked from action level to minor flood levels. No damages to downtown businesses/structures were reported. ||The Village of Pepin had pumped water off private property and roadways; the highway department reports assorted roadways damages throughout county including local, county, and state roadways due to culvert washouts and water over roads from melting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems, one over the northern Rockies, and another moving out into the southern Plains, merged over the central United States the weekend of March 9-10th. ||Initially, warmer air surged northward across the Upper Midwest and allowed for mostly rain and sleet at the beginning of the event across southern Minnesota. A dry layer in the boundary layer kept most of west central Wisconsin dry through Saturday afternoon, with the bulk of the precipitation Saturday night, and Sunday morning. ||Once the precipitation started, the precipitation quickly changed over to snow as the warm layer aloft eroded quicker, along with the added colder air moving in from the east. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elderly woman drowned near DeSoto (Vernon County) during the night of March 29th. The woman wandered away from her home near Blackhawk Park and was swept away by flood waters from the Mississippi River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly woman drowned in Mississippi River flood waters in the vicinity of Blackhawk Park.  Sometime during the night of March 29th into the early morning hours of March 30th, the woman wandered away from her home and was swept away by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across western Wisconsin from March 9th into the 10th with accumulations generally in the 3 to 4 inch range. The weight of this snow on top of the copious amounts that fell in February was too much for some buildings to withstand. Near Mondovi (Buffalo County), a roof over a large hog barn collapsed with a fire starting after the collapse that killed almost 4,000 hogs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across Buffalo County from March 9th into the 10th with accumulations generally in the 3 to 4 inch range. The weight of this snow, combined with the copious amounts that fell in February, caused the roof over a large hog barn near Mondovi to collapse. A fire that started after the roof collapsed killed almost 4,000 hogs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest measured snowfall totals in Marathon County were 11.6 inches in Wausau, 11.4 inches near Weston, and 8.5 inches in Spencer. The 1.81 inches of precipitation in Wausau breaks the previous precipitation record for April 11th of 1.49 inches that was also set in 1922.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over Colorado and slowly tracked into western Iowa over a two-day period. Repeated bands of snow and mixed precipitation tracked through southern Wisconsin from Wednesday, April 10 through Thursday, April 11, 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Sauk Prairie ambulance rolled over while responding to a different rollover crash on the morning of Thursday, April 11th. The first crash was in the town of Sumpter on Highway 12, just north of Highway Z. The ambulance rolled over on Highway 78, just south of Highway Z in Prairie Township. The crew was injured and there were no patients in the ambulance. The Sauk County Sheriff's office reported that the ambulance started to slip on slush and ice while negotiating a curve, was buffeted by strong winds, and slid onto the shoulder before it overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early spring storm system produced snow, sleet and freezing rain across western Wisconsin from April 10th into the 11th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches. Strong east winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts, brought down power lines across portions of Vernon and La Crosse Counties where enough freezing rain occurred to coat exposed surfaces. The highest reported snowfall total was 10.3 inches near Montana (Buffalo County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across Buffalo County. The highest reported total was 10.3 inches near Montana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 2 and 7 inches of snow fell across Vernon County. In addition to the snow, a period of sleet and freezing rain occurred that coated exposed surfaces with a glaze of ice. With the glaze of ice, between 20 and 25 power poles were brought down by the strong winds causing about 1,000 people in the Viroqua area to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late March along the Mississippi River continued through the month of April at La Crosse (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began in late March along the Mississippi River in La Crosse, continued through the entire month of April. The river first crested around two and half feet above the flood stage at 14.58 feet on April 2nd. The river crested a second time over two feet above the flood stage at 14.33 feet on April 23rd. Numerous parks, boat landings, campgrounds and some roads were closed because of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that passed south of Wisconsin combined with an upper level disturbance to produce heavy snow across much of the state. Many locations in the area set new daily snowfall records for February 12th including Green Bay (Brown Co.), Merrill (Lincoln Co.), Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), Marshfield (Wood Co.), Wausau (Marathon Co.), and Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.).||The highest snowfall totals included: 16.0 inches in Minocqua (Oneida Co.), in Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), and near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.); 15.7 inches in Wausau; 15.5 inches near Pella (Shawano Co.) and near Rib Mountain; 15.4 inches in New London (Outagamie Co.) and Wisconsin Rapids; and 15.0 inches in Florence (Florence Co.), in Plover (Portage Co.), and near Dancy (Marathon Co.).||The 15.7 inches of snow that fell at Wausau broke their record for the most snowfall from a single storm during the month of February. The old record was 13.5 inches, set in 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest recorded snowfall totals in Winnebago County were 9.0 inches at Oshkosh and at Neenah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As with previous storms this month of February, a classic winter storm moved from the Central Plains, northeast across the Midwest, and southern Great Lakes region from late Monday evening, February 11th, to Tuesday afternoon, February 12th. ||Snow began to fall in southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the late evening of Monday, February 11th, and continued through Tuesday afternoon in west central Wisconsin before weakening. The heaviest snow fell between midnight, and noon Tuesday. Up to six inches of snow fell during this time, with more snow during the afternoon, and early evening. The heaviest fell in Dunn, Chippewa and Eau Claire Counties where locally 8 to 10 inches fell. There were even higher amounts that fell just south and east of Chippewa and Eau Claire Counties.||The highest measured snowfall amounts were:|11.6 inches near Elk Mound. |11.0 inches west of Cornell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few local weather observers measured around 6 inches of snow across Pepin County. The heaviest occurred near Durand.

A fast-moving upper level disturbance moved across Wisconsin, dropping 6 to 9 inches of snow in a 12 to 18 hour period in parts of central, north-central, and northeast Wisconsin. The highest snow totals included 9.0 inches in Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 8.4 inches in Aurora (Florence Co.), 8.0 inches in Townsend (Oconto Co.), 7.8 inches at Rice Reservoir Number 2 (Lincoln Co.), 7.7 inches in Crandon (Forest Co.), and 7.5 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Oneida County were 7.5 inches in Rhinelander and 6.7 inches near Bradley and near Goodnow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures warmed into the 40s over the first few days of February which was enough to allow the ice to break up on some rivers. The ice break up created ice jams on the Kickapoo and Platte Rivers that lead to some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Platte River caused flooding to occur near Rockville. The ice jam formed after temperatures warmed into the 40s during the early part of February. The river crested over a foot above the flood state at 10.19 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures warmed into the 40s over the first few days of February which was enough to allow the ice to break up on some rivers. The ice break up created ice jams on the Kickapoo and Platte Rivers that lead to some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Kickapoo River caused flooding to occur in and around Gays Mills. The ice jam formed after temperatures warmed into the 40s during the early part of February. The river crested less than a half foot above the flood stage at 13.2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This snowfall event occurred in a small area due to a combination of elevated instability and mid-level forcing. ||At the surface, a large ridge of high pressure dominated the Upper Midwest during the morning of Tuesday, February, 5th. A dry and cold air mass was initially across the region, and most of the precipitation was not reaching the ground. As the forcing increased, regional radar noted banding in the reflectivity. The higher reflectivity promoted higher saturation rates and led to snow reaching the ground. By the late morning, and through the afternoon, surface observations noted low visibility and regional road conditions deteriorated rapidly as snowfall intensity rose to 1 to 1.5 inches per hour. ||The main band set up along and north of a line from Faribault, to Red Wing, to Eau Claire, Wisconsin. In this band, snowfall amounts  ranged from 7 to 10 inches. The heaviest occurred near Northfield, to Hastings in Minnesota, and from River Falls to Chippewa Falls in Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 8 inches of snow fell across Eau Claire County during the afternoon of Tuesday, February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening low pressure system moved from the southern Plains to central Lake Michigan, bringing heavy snow to northern and parts of central Wisconsin. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from eastern Kansas into southwestern Wisconsin May 8 into May 9. A rain/snow line did set up across northwest Wisconsin, leading to a sharp cutoff in snowfall amounts across the region. The areas that did see snow the night of the 8th had most of it melt during the day on the 9th with most of it being gone by the next morning. Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was seen along the South Shore of Lake Superior with 9.9 inches near Maple and 9.5 inches near Moquah.

A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to western Wisconsin from February 11th into the early morning hours of the 13th. The snow was heaviest along and north of Interstate 90 where totals of 12 to 20 inches were common. Overall, snowfall amounts ranged from 6 inches up to 20 inches. The highest reported total was 20 inches in Independence (Trempealeau County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Interstate 90 was shut down February 12th near Oakdale (Monroe County) where a State Trooper suffered injuries when the patrol car was rear-ended by a semi truck. Most schools were closed on February 12th including Western Technical College and Viterbo University.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 10 and 14 inches of snow fell across Jackson County. The highest reported total was 14.8 inches in Hatfield. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm dumped as much as 14 inches of snow across parts of western Wisconsin from February 23rd into the 24th. As the snow was winding down, the winds increased with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts of 45 to 55 mph. This created blizzard conditions for much of the 24th with major drifting, white-outs, and dangerous travel conditions across the region. The effects of the blizzard continued into the 25th when some schools were either closed or had a two hour late start. The weight of the accumulated snow caused two barns to collapse near Withee (Clark County), seven barns collapsed in Trempealeau County killing around 40 cows and part of a church roof collapsed in Osseo (Trempealeau County). The highest reported snowfall amount was 16 inches near Independence (Trempealeau County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 47 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Medford airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across La Crosse County. As the snow was ending, winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts that created drifting, reduced visibility and travel not being advised. The impacts from the storm caused some schools to start two hours late on February 25th. The highest reported snowfall total was 6 inches near New Amsterdam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly moving storm system affected the Upper Midwest with a band of heavy snowfall during the Sunday, February 10th. Initially, a band of snow developed around the Twin Cities, east into west central Wisconsin. This band of heavier snowfall led to 2 to 3 inches of snow by the mid morning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observers and trained spotters, measured between 5 and 7 inches of snow from Menomonie to Elk Mound.

Many locations in the area set new daily snowfall records for February 12th including Green Bay (Brown Co.), Merrill (Lincoln Co.), Appleton (Outagamie Co.), Sturgeon Bay (Door Co.), Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), Marshfield (Wood Co.), Wausau (Marathon Co.), and Wisconsin Rapids (Wood Co.).||The highest snowfall totals included: 16.0 inches in Minocqua (Oneida Co.), in Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), and near Spread Eagle (Florence Co.); 15.7 inches in Wausau; 15.5 inches near Pella (Shawano Co.) and near Rib Mountain; 15.4 inches in New London (Outagamie Co.) and Wisconsin Rapids; and 15.0 inches in Florence (Florence Co.), in Plover (Portage Co.), and near Dancy (Marathon Co.).||The 15.7 inches of snow that fell at Wausau broke their record for the most snowfall from a single storm during the month of February. The old record was 13.5 inches, set in 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest recorded snowfall totals in Outagamie County were 15.4 inches in New London and 10.2 inches in Appleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As with previous storms this month of February, a classic winter storm moved from the Central Plains, northeast across the Midwest, and southern Great Lakes region from late Monday evening, February 11th, to Tuesday afternoon, February 12th. ||Snow began to fall in southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the late evening of Monday, February 11th, and continued through Tuesday afternoon in west central Wisconsin before weakening. The heaviest snow fell between midnight, and noon Tuesday. Up to six inches of snow fell during this time, with more snow during the afternoon, and early evening. The heaviest fell in Dunn, Chippewa and Eau Claire Counties where locally 8 to 10 inches fell. There were even higher amounts that fell just south and east of Chippewa and Eau Claire Counties.||The highest measured snowfall amounts were:|11.6 inches near Elk Mound. |11.0 inches west of Cornell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers measured around 6 inches of snow across Barron County. Locally higher amounts were measured north and east of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon of June 27th. These storms produced some spotty wind damage with trees blow down in La Crosse (La Crosse County), Sparta (Monroe County) and Viroqua (Vernon County). A home southeast of Coon Valley (Vernon County) was hit by lightning and destroyed by the resulting fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in and just north of Sparta, which caused some power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of storms developed during the afternoon of Friday, June 14th near Eau Claire, Wisconsin. One storm became severe and produced damaging winds with several reports of blown down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pictures from social media showed large trees blown down from the University of Wisconsin, Eau Claire campus, south and east across the southern part of the city. A few trees landed on homes and caused damage to their roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced wind damage in parts of western Wisconsin during the mid to late afternoon on July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few residences along Rongholt Valley Road and County Road Z sustained wind damage from passing thunderstorms, including damage to trees and smaller buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the early morning hours of July 18th. These storms primarily produced locally heavy rains, but a couple of storms briefly became strong enough to drop some hail over northern Grant County. The largest reported hail was quarter sized between Woodman and Boscobel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell between Woodman and Boscobel.

Western Wisconsin sweltered under two days of excessive heat in the middle of July. Heat indices topped out between 100 and 110 on July 18th and 105 and 120 on July 19th. Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 118 from a mesonet station near Cataract (Monroe County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 100 to 115 were common across Vernon County on July 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 115 from the automated weather observing equipment at the Viroqua airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a locally heavy rain event in early July, some river flooding occurred along the Kickapoo River in Ontario (Vernon County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Kickapoo River out of its banks in Ontario. The river crested over two and a half feet above the flood stage at 18.69 feet on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Wisconsin brought a round of strong thunderstorms to the area during the early afternoon hours of Sunday, May 19, 2019. The storms evolved into a line and caused sporadic wind damage across portions of southeast Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts caused sporadic tree damage in parts of western Milwaukee. There was a report of a large tree with at least a 12 inch diameter base down 2 miles SSW of West Milwaukee. A large tree snapped at the base and there were several large branches down 2 miles ENE of Wauwatosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across portions of western Wisconsin during the afternoon of June 30th. These storms primarily produced damaging winds with the southern half of Grant County hit the hardest. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down with an estimated 20,000 customers without power. A 75 mph wind gust was measured by the automated weather observing equipment at the Platteville airport (Grant County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of trees and power lines were blown down across much of Grant County. The hardest hit areas were across the southern half of the county, including Cuba City which was hit particularly hard with damaging winds. 20,000 customers across the county were without power.

A thunderstorm along a cold front produced large hail in southern Sheboygan County.
A line of strong to severe thunderstorms along a warm front produced a couple areas of tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees and a wooden fence blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms along a warm front produced a couple areas of tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees snapped and uprooted in a couple neighborhoods in Kenosha. Some minor roof damage. Power lines down. A house fire related to downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of weak low pressure and its cold front brought a line of strong to severe thunderstorms to southern WI during the evening.
The passage of weak low pressure and its cold front brought a line of strong to severe thunderstorms to southern WI during the evening.

During the morning of Wednesday, April 17th, a large area of showers developed across southern Minnesota. Several embedded thunderstorms developed after sunrise and moved into west central Wisconsin. A very anomalous moist atmosphere was in place and rainfall was very efficient. One storm moved from Hastings Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin near River Falls. Rainfall rates were high, and some observers and weather sensors reported up to 1 and 2 inches per hour. By the afternoon as the precipitation slowly ended, a band of 2 to 4 inches of rain fell from near Prescott, to River Falls Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rain caused a driveway to wash out north of River Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of recent heavy rainfall, snow melt and very moist ground, led to a partial dam failure on Thursday, April 18th. ||The local Chippewa County Emergency Manager indicated that there was a partial failure of the Tilden Mill Dam, on Duncan Creek north of Chippewa Falls. There was a release of approximately 1500 cfs into the creek. ||Duncan Creek Campground, downstream from the dam, sustained some flood damage from the release of water at Tilden Mill Pond. Flood waters were high enough to reach some of the camper trailers and at least one unit camper was destroyed. It appeared to be a ���fifth wheel��� unit. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duncan Creek Campground, downstream from the dam, sustained some flood damage from the release of water at Tilden Mill Pond. Flood waters were high enough to reach some of the camper trailers and at least one unit camper was destroyed. It appeared to be a ���fifth wheel��� unit. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were multiple waves of precipitation that occurred with this major winter storm that lasted for more than two days.||The first wave of precipitation developed during the morning of Wednesday, April 10th where the initial band moved from southern Minnesota into west central Wisconsin by the afternoon and evening. This band of precipitation had thunder, sleet and heavy snow that produced several inches of snow across portions of west central Wisconsin. Most of the heavier precipitation tapered off, but light snow continued across portions of west central Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of Thursday, April 11th.||In addition to the precipitation Thursday morning, the next weather element developed. This weather element was a significant increase in the wind speeds, with sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph, with gusts of 40 mph or more across a wide area of west central Wisconsin. This created areas of blowing and drifting snow. The strong winds also contributed to damage of power poles. The local electric company indicated 13 power poles were snapped in half near New Richmond, and parts of I-94, west of Eau Claire, was closed due to numerous crashes. ||By Thursday afternoon, most of the heavier precipitation and thunderstorms had moved north and east of the area, with mainly light precipitation in the form of drizzle, light snow or sleet in small areas rotating northward as the main storm started to weaken and move overhead. In addition, wind speeds lessened.||The third wave of precipitation was associated with the main upper level system and changed the precipitation over to all snow by early Friday morning, April 12th. By the afternoon, most of the precipitation had ended, but residual light snow or flurries continued as the main system moved off to the east.||Snowfall totals as the storm ended ranged from 7 to 12 inches from Hudson to River Falls, eastward to Elk Mound, Chippewa Falls and Eau Claire. Lesser amounts were observed from Polk County, eastward to Rusk County. This area received between 2 to 6 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several waves of precipitation occurred across the county with light to moderate snow at the start, with a mixture of sleet, and snow during the overnight hours before transitioning to mainly light snow, drizzle Wednesday evening, and Thursday. Snowfall totals averaged 7 to 11 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to the near record high water levels on Lake Michigan, the Milwaukee River has also risen resulting in basement flooding in nearby restaurants and businesses. The Milwaukee Health Department ordered the temporary closure of two restaurants. The basement flooding, or the use pumps for mitigation, will continue through the summer or beyond until the lake and river levels drop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to the near record high water levels on Lake Michigan, the Milwaukee River has also risen resulting in basement flooding in nearby restaurants and businesses. The Milwaukee Health Department ordered the temporary closure of two restaurants. The basement flooding or the use pumps for mitigation will continue through the summer and beyond until the lake and river levels drop.

By the afternoon, most of the precipitation had ended, but residual light snow or flurries continued as the main system moved off to the east.||Snowfall totals as the storm ended ranged from 7 to 12 inches from Hudson to River Falls, eastward to Elk Mound, Chippewa Falls and Eau Claire. Lesser amounts were observed from Polk County, eastward to Rusk County. This area received between 2 to 6 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several waves of precipitation occurred across the county with light to moderate snow at the start, with a mixture of sleet, and snow during the overnight hours before transitioning to mainly light snow, drizzle Wednesday evening, and Thursday. Snowfall totals averaged 7 to 10 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of southwest Wisconsin during the evening of August 13th. Some of these storms became strong enough to produce damaging winds in Prairie du Chien (Crawford County). The automated weather observing equipment at the airport measured a 66 mph wind gust and trees and power lines were blown down across the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Prairie du Chien airport measured a 66 mph wind gust. Trees and power lines were blown down in parts of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of June 14, 2019, thunderstorms moved over Price County. One of the storms produced a tornado which began 4.1 miles west-northwest of Kennan and ended 2.2 miles west-northwest of Ogema. Sporadic damage was reported which is consistent with an EF-0 tornado with peak winds of 65-75 mph. The tornado was well-documented with numerous photos and videos during its life.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began at 654 PM CDT just east of Rusk Road and north of US Highway 8 in a wooded area and moved toward the east-southeast. Large hardwood branches were broken on County Road J just north of US Highway 8 northwest of Kennan. It crossed US Highway 8 near County Highway N on the northeast side of Kennan with no visible damage. The tornado crossed Midway Road east of Woodlawn Road to the west-southwest of Catawba and flipped a chicken coop and removed a roof from an outbuilding. It then continued east-southeast and crossed County Road I between Main Street and Moonshine Alley where a farmstead lost shingles off a single family home and several trees were damaged. It was at this location that the tornado reached its peak intensity. The tornado continued to move toward the east-southeast with little damage observed until it crossed County Road O and Otteson Road west of Ogema. A softwood tree and several large branches were broken as it crossed County Road O. As the tornado crossed Otteson Road it dished in overhead doors attached to an outbuilding, rolled a farm tractor onto its side, pushed a shed several yards, and partially removed the shingles from the same shed. The tornado lifted at 736 PM CDT in a wooded area south of Otteson Road and west of 10 Crossing Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated warm front pushing into northwest Wisconsin the morning of the 18th became the focal point for the development of a few isolated supercells around the midday hours. Storms that fired near the front were in a very unstable and highly sheared environment. One storm produced half-dollar size hail before dissipating while another made a right turn and dropped a brief tornado before quickly dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began east of the intersection of Sand Road and Cabin Lane. The damage path was picked up the where it crossed Cabin Lane several hundred yards west of Sand Road.  Numerous uprooted and broken softwood trees were noted on either side of Cabin Lane at that location. The damage at that location was consistent with high-end EF-0 wind speeds of 75 to 85 mph. The tornado continued east-southeast and strengthened to EF-1 intensity south of the intersection of Sand Road and Cabin Lane. Extensive tree damage was noted with nearly all trees in the area broken or uprooted. This damage seemed consistent with winds of 85 to 95 mph. From that location to a point just west of Schaefer Road a path was cut through the trees which was approximately 80 yards wide and approximately one-half mile in length. All the trees in this area were either broken or uprooted and the damage was consistent with winds of 100 to 110 mph. The tornado weakened quickly after crossing Schaefer Road. A few broken branches and uprooted trees were noted along Jackson Road and locations farther east. It seems the tornado lifted between Schaefer Road and Jackson Road, which is where the end of the track was approximated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure area brought a prolonged period of light to moderate snow to southern WI from the evening of October 30th through much of October 31st. Due to the relatively mild air temperatures and road temperatures, most major roadways remained wet while secondary roads did have some minor snow accumulation. Overall, 4 to 8 inches of wet snow fell over southern WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure approached the region through the day on Monday, October 21st, producing increasingly strong east to northeast winds. The winds increased through the day, and were especially strong coming off Lake Superior into the western South Shore area. Peak wind speeds topped 70 MPH at some of the bridges and waves caused damage along many of the lake shore areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from Lake Superior was being upstream be the strong winds on the Flagg River in Port Wing and was overtopping the Washington Avenue bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved from central Minnesota the morning of the 4th to northeastern Wisconsin by the morning of the 5th. Slow moving showers and thunderstorms develop near this low across northwest Wisconsin and dropped 1 to 3 inches of rainfall in a span of a few hours leading to isolated areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported after 1.72 inches of rain fell in about an hour and 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved from central Minnesota the morning of the 4th to northeastern Wisconsin by the morning of the 5th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report and photos of flash flooding was received via social media. Cars were driving through 6 to 12 inches of water along Wisconsin Highway 35 at Djock Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved from central Minnesota the morning of the 4th to northeastern Wisconsin by the morning of the 5th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over southern Manitoba the morning of the 15th moved to near southern Hudson Bay by the morning of the 16th. A cold front associated with this low moved across the Northland during the afternoon and evening hours of the 15th. A warm front had moved through on the previous day brought a warm and unstable airmass in its wake. The cold front then worked with this airmass to create severe storms from northeast Minnesota into northwest Wisconsin and western Lake Superior. Damaging winds accounted for most of the reports with these storms, although there was some marginally severe hail as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small branches and leaves were stripped off trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milwaukee River at Cedarburg reached moderate flood stage, cresting at 13 feet during the late morning on October 3rd. Floodwaters affect Island Drive and Shoreland Parkway in the Mequon area. Water is over Elm St., Green Bay Rd., North Cedarburg Rd., and portions of the village park in Thiensville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The east branch of the Pecatonica River at Blanchardville reached minor flood stage, cresting at 13.75 feet the early afternoon of October 3rd.  McKellar Park in Blanchardville is completely flooded including the swimming pool and tennis courts. Oak St., highway H and Water St. are closed near the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring Creek at Lodi reached moderate flood stage, cresting at 7.3 feet the morning of October 2nd. Floodwaters affect Goeres Park including the pool, and Habermann Park including the baseball diamond. Floodwaters approach the Public Works garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Bayshore Road was often under water due to high lake levels.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around the Kewaunee Marina were often under water due to high lake levels.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees east of Minoqua. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.
