EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of strong east to southeast onshore winds caused high waves at the Lake Michigan shoreline for several hours.  These conditions resulted in an enhanced risk of lakeshore erosion and flooding. The strongest onshore winds occurred between 10 am CDT and 4 pm CDT Monday, October 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pershing Park Drive in the City of Racine was closed due to rocks and debris being thrown onto the roadways by high waves.  The gravel parking area adjacent to the park will need to be re-graded due to the debris. Time included is when the duration of the strongest onshore winds at the Racine airport (KRAC).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of high lake levels, northeasterly winds, and recent rainfall resulted in lakeshore flooding along the southern Bay of Green Bay during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lakeshore flooding impacted several properties near Sunset Beach Lane, Sunset Beach Road, Longtail Beach Lane, Resort Road and Cottage Row in Suamico. Residents were advised to evacuate homes as water rose to impassable conditions on the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High lake levels and northeasterly winds once again resulted in lakeshore flooding along the southern Bay of Green Bay during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lakeshore flooding impacted several properties near Sunset Beach Lane, Sunset Beach Road, Longtail Beach Lane, Resort Road and Cottage Row in Suamico. Roadways were once again impassible due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Black Earth Creek at Mazomanie reached minor flood stage, cresting at 10.95 feet the afternoon of October 2nd. Floodwaters affect Curtiss Street and other streets on the northeast side of Mazomanie. There is also general lowland flooding in the Mazomanie area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Baraboo River at Baraboo reached minor flood stage, cresting at 18.23 feet the morning of October 4th. Tritz Rd. was flooded and closed near the intersection with highway 33.  Floodwaters affected a portion of the parking lot of a trailer rental business just east of the highway 33 bridge over the Baraboo River. A nearby wayside park was also partially flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The south branch of the Rock River at Waupun reached minor flood stage, cresting at 7.83 feet by late morning on October 2nd. Water reaches the support beams underneath the Fond du Lac St. bridge and it is closed. Gateway Dr. is flooded and closed. Water starts to over top the dam at Shaler Park. County Park Rd. is closed at the bridge as the river flows around the north end of bridge. Various parks and associated roads are closed along the river.

Floodwaters affect Riverside Park on the north side of Janesville and South River Road on the south side of Janesville. Floodwaters affect a home on South Christian Road on the east side of the river in the Afton area. There is extensive lowland and agricultural land flooding in the Afton and Janesville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rain in early October caused the Rock River to flood much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Lake Koshkonong reached minor flood stage, cresting at 11 feet the late afternoon of October 15th. There is widespread flooding of roads and docks in the Town of Koshkonong and the Town of Sumner, including Blackhawk Island Road and Oxbow Bend Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rain in early October caused the Rock River to flood much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Newville reached minor flood stage, cresting at 7.15 feet the late evening of October 14th. Floodwaters surround some homes near East Mallwood Drive and are into yards and roads across eastern Newville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated warm front pushing into northwest Wisconsin the morning of the 18th became the focal point for the development of a few isolated supercells around the midday hours. Storms that fired near the front were in a very unstable and highly sheared environment. One storm produced half-dollar size hail before dissipating while another made a right turn and dropped a brief tornado before quickly dissipating.

An area of low pressure over southern Manitoba the morning of the 15th moved to near southern Hudson Bay by the morning of the 16th. A cold front associated with this low moved across the Northland during the afternoon and evening hours of the 15th. A warm front had moved through on the previous day brought a warm and unstable airmass in its wake. The cold front then worked with this airmass to create severe storms from northeast Minnesota into northwest Wisconsin and western Lake Superior. Damaging winds accounted for most of the reports with these storms, although there was some marginally severe hail as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2-inch branch was broken from a tree in the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of southeast Gales and high lake levels caused lakeshore flooding across eastward facing beaches on the Bay of Green Bay and Lake Michigan during the afternoon hours.

A historical marker, the Christmas Tree Ship Point suffered significant damage due to the high waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across much of central, north central, and eastern Wisconsin during the evening hours of July 19, 2019. Widespread tree and power line damage was reported from central Wisconsin into the Fox Valley and lake shore. WPS reported over 50,000 outages at the height of the storm.||The worst damage was associated with a macroburst, a large downburst of straight-line winds that affected a large swath from Pelican Lake in Oneida County, southeast through Langlade and Oconto counties. Hundreds of thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted, resulting in damage to dozens of homes and cottages. The damage path was about 60 miles long and up to 10 miles wide at times. Winds were likely near 100 mph in the hardest hit areas near Lily in northeast Langlade County and near the Langlade-Oconto County line southwest of Boot Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Colby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive day, a line of powerful thunderstorms moved across portions of central and east-central Wisconsin. The worst damage occurred from Wood/Portage counties eastward into the Fox Valley and lakeshore. Trees were uprooted or snapped and several structures were damaged as a result of straight-line winds and six weak, fast-moving tornadoes.||There was also a fatality due to drowning in Keshena (Menominee Co.) from abnormally fast flows on the Wolf River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado damaged a few tree limbs (DI 27,28, DOD 2) and took some shingles and siding off a house (DI 2, DOD 2) northeast of Greenville. A power pole was also damaged. Peak winds were estimated near 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin during the late afternoon and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that produced large hail in the Hayward Lakes area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in the storm.

WPS reported over 50,000 outages at the height of the storm.||The worst damage was associated with a macroburst, a large downburst of straight-line winds that affected a large swath from Pelican Lake in Oneida County, southeast through Langlade and Oconto counties. Hundreds of thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted, resulting in damage to dozens of homes and cottages. The damage path was about 60 miles long and up to 10 miles wide at times. Winds were likely near 100 mph in the hardest hit areas near Lily in northeast Langlade County and near the Langlade-Oconto County line southwest of Boot Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in Crivitz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive day, a line of powerful thunderstorms moved across portions of central and east-central Wisconsin. The worst damage occurred from Wood/Portage counties eastward into the Fox Valley and lakeshore. Trees were uprooted or snapped and several structures were damaged as a result of straight-line winds and six weak, fast-moving tornadoes.||There was also a fatality due to drowning in Keshena (Menominee Co.) from abnormally fast flows on the Wolf River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous tree limbs and one tree, larger than a foot in diameter, in Almond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front started moving across the Northland during the early morning hours and exited by the early afternoon. A few storms developed ahead of the front, but remained below severe limits. One of the storms in northwest Wisconsin produced sub-severe hail before exiting the CWA just before 1PM.

Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the late evening of July 18th into the early morning hours of July 19th. As the initial round of storms moved through, a brief tornado touched down northeast of Westby (Vernon County). The tornado destroyed a barn, flipped a trailer and damaged numerous trees. A fatal accident occurred near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) during the storms. Authorities indicated that weather was likely a factor in a head-on two vehicle crash. Rainfall totals from the storms ranged from 3 to 5 inches across portions of La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties that resulted in some flash flooding. Campers had to be evacuated from the Kickapoo Valley Reserve. Flood waters covered County Road P north of Coon Valley (Vernon County) and east of Westby (Vernon County) where a mudslide also occurred. U.S. Highway 14 was closed near Coon Valley where flood waters from Coon Creek covered the road.  The initial storms also produced some 60 mph wind gusts near Mindoro (La Crosse County) and Tomah (Monroe County) and blew down trees near Barre Mills (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Kickapoo River out of its banks in Steuben. The river crested almost two feet above the flood stage at 13.81 feet on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin during the late afternoon and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that produced large hail in the Hayward Lakes area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eight inch diameter tree was knocked down onto a power line approximately 5 miles north of Catawba on County Road J.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across northwest Wisconsin the evening of the 12th. A few storms developed ahead of the front with one storm producing nickel-sized hail.

A warm front set up over the region during the early morning hours of the 30th. Torrential rainfall was reported overnight leading to saturated soils in the area. Additional showers and thunderstorms moved over these same areas during the late morning and early afternoon hours and led to some isolated flash flooding in Douglas County Wisconsin where water over the roadway led to a few closed roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was received showing a few inches of water over County Highway B in Foxboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed ahead of a cold front moving across the region during the evening of the 24th in Northwest Wisconsin. Mainly clear skies and daytime heating led to instability across the area, but other severe ingredients were lacking and most of the storms that did develop were sub-severe. A few of the storms did produce some low-end severe hail with the largest being ping pong ball-sized.
The passage of a weak cold front led to several severe thunderstorms over southeast Wisconsin during the afternoon. Scattered tree damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees down, some 16 inches or greater in diameter north of downtown Racine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms along a stationary front brought heavy rain, flash flooding, and tree damage to a small portion of Sauk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several branches and trees blown down from Reedsburg to La Valle. A large pine tree fell onto two vehicles in Reedsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms along a stationary front brought heavy rain, flash flooding, and tree damage to a small portion of Sauk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of creeks and some roads from La Valle to Reedsburg after several inches of rain. A road washout occurred at the intersection of highway 33 and Twin Pine Rd. Babb Creek flooded the backyards of residential homes in Reedsburg. Some basement flooding occurred in Reedsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms near a warm front moved across south central WI the late evening of July 18th into the early morning of July 19th. Sauk County was impacted the most by damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees down in Spring Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 19th, a complex of severe thunderstorms moved across north-central Wisconsin. These storms produced two short-lived tornadoes along with damaging winds. The first tornado touched down west of Withee (Clark County) and damaged a farmstead and trees. The second tornado occurred north of Withee where a residence and trees were damaged. Significant wind damage occurred from Owen (Clark County) east into Abbotsford (Clark County) where barns, silos, trees and power lines were blown down. A federal disaster declaration was made for Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred north of Withee. The tornado touched down in a field, crossed Center Road before dissipating in a wooded area. Trees were blown onto a mobile home and numerous power poles along Center Road were snapped or bent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin on July 20th. The first round moved across during the morning and produced two tornadoes. The first tornado touched down in far southeast Trempealeau County and damaged corn and trees. The second tornado occurred northeast of North Bend (Jackson County) with the damage again limited to corn and trees. This round of storms also blew down trees from near Galesville (Trempealeau County) and Holmen (La Crosse County) northeast to Spencer (Clark County). The second round of storms moved across during the afternoon and blew down trees near Rockland (La Crosse County) and Rockbridge (Richland County). Around 7,000 customers lost power in La Crosse County after the storms moved through. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide State of Emergency. A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down across the northern part of La Crosse County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Marshfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving across the waters of Green Bay produced seiche conditions in southeast Oconto County and northern Brown County during the evening of July 24th. Gusty winds and lake levels that started out high combined to exacerbate problems caused by the seiche. The water level at the mouth of the Fox River in Green Bay (Brown Co.) rose more than a foot and a half during the evening.||At least one business in Little Suamico (Oconto Co.) flooded and water spilled over the breakwater in Oconto (Oconto Co.). Police in Suamico (Brown Co.) advised residents to evacuate homes on Longtail Beach Lane as water rose to impassable conditions on the roadway. High water on Duck Creek necessitated closure of a nearby road and there was flooding at Barney Williams Park in Howard (Brown Co.). Boat docks at a business on the Fox River in Green Bay broke loose and had to be redirected by the U.S. Coast Guard and the Green Bay Fire Department to keep them from hitting a ship that was docked farther downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A seiche caused flooding on Green Bay that impacted the shoreline in northern Brown County.  Residents were advised to evacuate homes on Longtail Beach Lane as water rose to impassable conditions on the roadway. High water on Duck Creek caused closure of a road and flooding of a park in Howard. Docks at a business in Green Bay broke loose and floated downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed ahead of a cold front moving across northern Minnesota on the afternoon of the 26th. These storms contained large hail and damaging wind gusts as they moved across the Minnesota Arrowhead. The storms remained severe as they moved over western Lake Superior and across the Bayfield Peninsula of northwestern Wisconsin where hail to tennis ball-size was reported along with downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were knocked down along Wisconsin 13 between Cornucopia and Herbster.

An area of low pressure spread a large area of rain over the Northland on the 28th. A few storms were embedded within the larger area of showers and we able to tap into stronger winds aloft and bring them to the surface, resulting in a few reports of downed trees in Burnett County. Otherwise, the storms did not produce anything else of note.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were knocked down along Birch Haven Road.

Mainly clear skies and daytime heating led to instability across the area, but other severe ingredients were lacking and most of the storms that did develop were sub-severe. A few of the storms did produce some low-end severe hail with the largest being ping pong ball-sized.

An area of low pressure spread a large area of rain over the Northland on the 28th. A few storms were embedded within the larger area of showers and we able to tap into stronger winds aloft and bring them to the surface, resulting in a few reports of downed trees in Burnett County. Otherwise, the storms did not produce anything else of note.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed near the intersection of French Road and Glendenning Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across much of central, north central, and eastern Wisconsin during the evening hours of July 19, 2019. Widespread tree and power line damage was reported from central Wisconsin into the Fox Valley and lake shore. WPS reported over 50,000 outages at the height of the storm.||The worst damage was associated with a macroburst, a large downburst of straight-line winds that affected a large swath from Pelican Lake in Oneida County, southeast through Langlade and Oconto counties. Hundreds of thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted, resulting in damage to dozens of homes and cottages. The damage path was about 60 miles long and up to 10 miles wide at times. Winds were likely near 100 mph in the hardest hit areas near Lily in northeast Langlade County and near the Langlade-Oconto County line southwest of Boot Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Brown County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms that went through the Twin Cities late Monday afternoon, and early evening, moved across portions of Barron and Rusk Counties and produced straight-line wind damage. The most significant occurred north of Bruce where numerous trees were uprooted or blown down. Isolated wind damage occurred early near Chippewa Falls where a few storm developed near Eau Claire and moved northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down on the east side of Lake Wissota, in Chippewa Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tornadic storms that went through portions of southern Minnesota, moved into west central Wisconsin and produced a pair of tornadoes in Polk and Barron Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado hit a large storage shed, taking off part of its roof and blowing in garage doors.  It also knocked over a few trees, and entered a corn field, where it dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tornadic storms that went through portions of southern Minnesota, moved into west central Wisconsin and produced a pair of tornadoes in Polk and Barron Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the southwest side of Bone Lake and moved north-northeast, knocking down hundreds of trees, some of which landed on houses, sheds and vehicles. The tornado also hit several farms, taking the roof off a barn, machine shed, and outbuildings, and knocking over a silo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of showers and storms during the early morning hours of September 12th created some flash flooding across portions of Grant County. Near Beetown, a mudslide occurred on County Highway U and a township road was closed because of water over it. High water also occurred along State Highway 35 near the intersection of State Highway 133 in Bloomington. Radar estimates indicate between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused a mudslide to occur south of Beetown along County Highway U. A township road northeast of Beetown was closed because of flood waters from the Platte River going over the road. High water also occurred along State Highway 35 near the intersection with State Highway 133 in Bloomington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of September 12th. These storms dropped 3 to 4 inches of rain on the region and with already saturated soils, flash flooding occurred across parts of Grant and Crawford Counties. Across the northwest portion of Grant County, nearly every township road was closed across three townships prompting two school districts to cancel classes on the 13th. In Crawford County, State Highway 35 was closed between Prairie du Chien and Ferryville and U.S. Highway 61 near the Wisconsin River was also closed because of debris on the road. In Prairie du Chien, the mayor declared a state of emergency after numerous homes were damaged by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused flash flooding to occur across the southern portion of Crawford County. State Highway 35 was closed between Prairie du Chien and Ferryville because of debris washed down from the nearby bluffs and onto the road. Extensive damage was done to a road construction project just north of Prairie du Chien. U.S. Highway 61 was also closed just north of the Wisconsin River because of debris from the nearby bluffside. Numerous homes in Prairie du Chien sustained damage from the flooding. Because of the large amount of mud that flowed into parts of the city, some residents were unable to leave their neighborhoods until city crews were able to clear the streets. A state of emergency was declared by the mayor. Creeks went out of their banks near Mt. Zion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the early morning hours of September 15th. These storms dropped 2 to 3 inches of rain on already saturated soils and the resulting runoff produced some flooding across Grant County. Both the Grant and Platte Rivers went out of their banks prompting officials to close some roads in the vicinity of Beetown, Burton and Tennyson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed both the Grant and Platte Rivers out of their banks. Flooding along these rivers sent water over some roads in the vicinity of Beetown, Burton and Tennyson, prompting officials to close the roads for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of severe storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the evening of September 24th. These storms produced a tornado in Clark County that started southwest of Greenwood and ended west of Loyal. The tornado damaged several farm buildings, silos and trees along its path. High winds from the storms blew down trees from Alma and Modena (Buffalo County), east through Eleva and Osseo (Trempealeau County) to near Humbird (Clark County). The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado tracked across rural sections of central Clark County. The tornado touched down near the Black River southwest of Greenwood and then moved northeast before dissipating west of Loyal. Along its path, the tornado damaged trees, barns, silos, and crops. At least three cows were killed at one farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along a warm front produced hail up to nickel size in east-central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Appleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado formed under a shower over western Waushara County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, thin, weak tornado touched down in an open field between Plainfield and Hancock. No damage was reported, but dust and a few small branches were lofted into the air by the funnel (DI 28, DOD 1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved slowly across portions of southwest Wisconsin during the early morning hours of July 1st. These storms dumped between 3 and 5 inches of rain over the northeast portions of Grant County, which resulted in some flash flooding. A mudslide occurred on County Highway M south of Blue River and water covered County Highway B southeast of Ellenboro. A mudslide near Glen Haven covered some railroad tracks and caused a train derailment to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dropped between 3 and 5 inches of rain across the northeast portion of Grant County. Runoff from the rain caused a mudslide to occur along County Highway M south of Blue River and water covered County Highway B southeast of Ellenboro. A mudslide near Glen Haven covered some railroad tracks resulting in a train derailment. Nine cars derailed with one partially going into the Mississippi River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the late evening of July 18th into the early morning hours of July 19th. As the initial round of storms moved through, a brief tornado touched down northeast of Westby (Vernon County). The tornado destroyed a barn, flipped a trailer and damaged numerous trees. A fatal accident occurred near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) during the storms. Authorities indicated that weather was likely a factor in a head-on two vehicle crash. Rainfall totals from the storms ranged from 3 to 5 inches across portions of La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties that resulted in some flash flooding. Campers had to be evacuated from the Kickapoo Valley Reserve. Flood waters covered County Road P north of Coon Valley (Vernon County) and east of Westby (Vernon County) where a mudslide also occurred. U.S. Highway 14 was closed near Coon Valley where flood waters from Coon Creek covered the road.  The initial storms also produced some 60 mph wind gusts near Mindoro (La Crosse County) and Tomah (Monroe County) and blew down trees near Barre Mills (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northbound lane of County Highway YY was blocked east of Barre Mills when trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin on July 20th. The first round moved across during the morning and produced two tornadoes. The first tornado touched down in far southeast Trempealeau County and damaged corn and trees. The second tornado occurred northeast of North Bend (Jackson County) with the damage again limited to corn and trees. This round of storms also blew down trees from near Galesville (Trempealeau County) and Holmen (La Crosse County) northeast to Spencer (Clark County). The second round of storms moved across during the afternoon and blew down trees near Rockland (La Crosse County) and Rockbridge (Richland County). Around 7,000 customers lost power in La Crosse County after the storms moved through. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide State of Emergency. A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in various places across the western part of the county.

This round of storms also blew down trees from near Galesville (Trempealeau County) and Holmen (La Crosse County) northeast to Spencer (Clark County). The second round of storms moved across during the afternoon and blew down trees near Rockland (La Crosse County) and Rockbridge (Richland County). Around 7,000 customers lost power in La Crosse County after the storms moved through. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide State of Emergency. A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down north of Blair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive day, a line of powerful thunderstorms moved across portions of central and east-central Wisconsin. The worst damage occurred from Wood/Portage counties eastward into the Fox Valley and lakeshore. Trees were uprooted or snapped and several structures were damaged as a result of straight-line winds and six weak, fast-moving tornadoes.||There was also a fatality due to drowning in Keshena (Menominee Co.) from abnormally fast flows on the Wolf River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees in Stangelville. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 19th, a complex of severe thunderstorms moved across north-central Wisconsin. These storms produced two short-lived tornadoes along with damaging winds. The first tornado touched down west of Withee (Clark County) and damaged a farmstead and trees. The second tornado occurred north of Withee where a residence and trees were damaged. Significant wind damage occurred from Owen (Clark County) east into Abbotsford (Clark County) where barns, silos, trees and power lines were blown down. A federal disaster declaration was made for Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph was measured by a private weather station southwest of Stetsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving across the waters of Green Bay produced seiche conditions in southeast Oconto County and northern Brown County during the evening of July 24th. Gusty winds and lake levels that started out high combined to exacerbate problems caused by the seiche. The water level at the mouth of the Fox River in Green Bay (Brown Co.) rose more than a foot and a half during the evening.||At least one business in Little Suamico (Oconto Co.) flooded and water spilled over the breakwater in Oconto (Oconto Co.). Police in Suamico (Brown Co.) advised residents to evacuate homes on Longtail Beach Lane as water rose to impassable conditions on the roadway. High water on Duck Creek necessitated closure of a nearby road and there was flooding at Barney Williams Park in Howard (Brown Co.). Boat docks at a business on the Fox River in Green Bay broke loose and had to be redirected by the U.S. Coast Guard and the Green Bay Fire Department to keep them from hitting a ship that was docked farther downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Green Bay impacted southern Oconto County. At least one business flooded in Little Suamico and water spilled over the breakwall in Oconto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across much of central, north central, and eastern Wisconsin during the evening hours of July 19, 2019. Widespread tree and power line damage was reported from central Wisconsin into the Fox Valley and lake shore. WPS reported over 50,000 outages at the height of the storm.||The worst damage was associated with a macroburst, a large downburst of straight-line winds that affected a large swath from Pelican Lake in Oneida County, southeast through Langlade and Oconto counties. Hundreds of thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted, resulting in damage to dozens of homes and cottages. The damage path was about 60 miles long and up to 10 miles wide at times. Winds were likely near 100 mph in the hardest hit areas near Lily in northeast Langlade County and near the Langlade-Oconto County line southwest of Boot Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped and uprooted tens of thousands of trees across northern Langlade County. One hundred thirty-five structures in the county sustained damage. The hardest hit area was near Lily.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Jefferson reached moderate flood stage, cresting at 11 feet the morning of October 12th. Floodwaters affect low lying areas of residential and business property in Jefferson. Water approaches the intersection of Main St. and E. Dane St.  The retention pond at this intersection is full. South Center Ave. near the river is flooded and Riverview Drive near the wastewater plant is flooded. Riverfront Park is flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Turtle Creek at Beloit reached minor flood stage, cresting at 8.39 feet at noon on October 3rd. Colley Road is closed between Sager Lane and Brewster Avenue. Floodwaters are in the backyards of some homes on East Ridge Road, Moccasin Drive, Boulder Lane, Rockfence Lane, Stonehedge Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin during the late afternoon and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that produced large hail in the Hayward Lakes area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upwards of 60 to 80 trees were reported down with some on power lines leading to power outages.

A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin during the late afternoon and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that produced large hail in the Hayward Lakes area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was covering the ground. Most of the stones were quarter-sized, but the largest were half dollar-sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across much of central, north central, and eastern Wisconsin during the evening hours of July 19, 2019. Widespread tree and power line damage was reported from central Wisconsin into the Fox Valley and lake shore. WPS reported over 50,000 outages at the height of the storm.||The worst damage was associated with a macroburst, a large downburst of straight-line winds that affected a large swath from Pelican Lake in Oneida County, southeast through Langlade and Oconto counties. Hundreds of thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted, resulting in damage to dozens of homes and cottages. The damage path was about 60 miles long and up to 10 miles wide at times. Winds were likely near 100 mph in the hardest hit areas near Lily in northeast Langlade County and near the Langlade-Oconto County line southwest of Boot Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail and heavy rain fell in Rhinelander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive day, a line of powerful thunderstorms moved across portions of central and east-central Wisconsin. The worst damage occurred from Wood/Portage counties eastward into the Fox Valley and lakeshore. Trees were uprooted or snapped and several structures were damaged as a result of straight-line winds and six weak, fast-moving tornadoes.||There was also a fatality due to drowning in Keshena (Menominee Co.) from abnormally fast flows on the Wolf River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed south of Waupaca and moved east-northeast to near White Lake. The tornado damaged siding and shingles on six homes (DI 2, DOD 2) and an apartment in a new subdivision near the Waupaca Airport. The garage door of a newer home was blown completely in, causing damage to the roof of the house (DI 2, DOD 4). Another house had a small part of its roof heavily damaged. Numerous trees were also damaged along the path. Peak winds were estimated around 90 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused street flooding at Circle Drive and Allied Street in Ashwaubenon, where the water was up to the headlights of a full size pickup truck. Flash flooding also impacted a nearby bowling alley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several inches of rain on October 1-2, with the passage of a slow moving cold front, many rivers reached flood stage with some flooding that continued well into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sugar River at Brodhead reached minor flood stage, cresting at 7.53 feet the evening of October 4th. Some roads are flooded about 6 miles downstream in Avon Township of Rock County. In the Avon Bottoms area of Rock County, flooding is occurring in Sugar River Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of rain in early October caused the Rock River to flood much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Fort Atkinson reached minor flood stage, cresting at 16.83 feet the afternoon of October 12th. The following roads in the Fort Atkinson area are flooded and closed: Sinissippi drive, Vets Lane, Bark River Road from Kutz Road to Rock River Road.  Rock River Road from Highway 106 to the Fort Atkinson city limits. The river walkway in Fort Atkinson becomes flooded and is closed.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved slowly across portions of southwest Wisconsin during the early morning hours of July 1st. These storms dumped between 3 and 5 inches of rain over the northeast portions of Grant County, which resulted in some flash flooding. A mudslide occurred on County Highway M south of Blue River and water covered County Highway B southeast of Ellenboro. A mudslide near Glen Haven covered some railroad tracks and caused a train derailment to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After thunderstorms dropped between 2 and 5 inches of rain across much of Grant County, several rivers and streams flooded including the Little Platte, Platte and Grant rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the late evening of July 18th into the early morning hours of July 19th. As the initial round of storms moved through, a brief tornado touched down northeast of Westby (Vernon County). The tornado destroyed a barn, flipped a trailer and damaged numerous trees. A fatal accident occurred near Whitehall (Trempealeau County) during the storms. Authorities indicated that weather was likely a factor in a head-on two vehicle crash. Rainfall totals from the storms ranged from 3 to 5 inches across portions of La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties that resulted in some flash flooding. Campers had to be evacuated from the Kickapoo Valley Reserve. Flood waters covered County Road P north of Coon Valley (Vernon County) and east of Westby (Vernon County) where a mudslide also occurred. U.S. Highway 14 was closed near Coon Valley where flood waters from Coon Creek covered the road.  The initial storms also produced some 60 mph wind gusts near Mindoro (La Crosse County) and Tomah (Monroe County) and blew down trees near Barre Mills (La Crosse County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred near Mindoro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 19th, a complex of severe thunderstorms moved across north-central Wisconsin. These storms produced two short-lived tornadoes along with damaging winds. The first tornado touched down west of Withee (Clark County) and damaged a farmstead and trees. The second tornado occurred north of Withee where a residence and trees were damaged. Significant wind damage occurred from Owen (Clark County) east into Abbotsford (Clark County) where barns, silos, trees and power lines were blown down. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin on July 20th. The first round moved across during the morning and produced two tornadoes. The first tornado touched down in far southeast Trempealeau County and damaged corn and trees. The second tornado occurred northeast of North Bend (Jackson County) with the damage again limited to corn and trees. This round of storms also blew down trees from near Galesville (Trempealeau County) and Holmen (La Crosse County) northeast to Spencer (Clark County). The second round of storms moved across during the afternoon and blew down trees near Rockland (La Crosse County) and Rockbridge (Richland County). Around 7,000 customers lost power in La Crosse County after the storms moved through. The Governor of Wisconsin declared a statewide State of Emergency. A federal disaster declaration was made for La Crosse, Monroe and Vernon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved across the far southwest corner of Jackson County northeast of North Bend. The tornado touched down in a small wooden area, moved across State Highway 54 before dissipating in another wooded area. Damage was limited to trees and some corn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the day on the 19th. Ahead of the front was a very moist and extremely unstable air mass along with a warm front. Strong wind shear was also in place, especially in the vicinity of the warm front and with a low-level jet pushing into the area as well. These ingredients led to storm development across central Minnesota by mid-afternoon with one storm becoming a dominant supercell that produced very large hail over a wide area, with some of that large hail being driven by winds in excess of 60 mph. This storm then crossed into Wisconsin during the late afternoon and continued to produce large hail and sporadic wind damage, along with a few funnel clouds. Heading into the evening hours, this storm evolved upscale into a large MCS that caused large amounts of damage across parts of Wisconsin. Additional storms were seen ahead of the large supercell that produced large hail in the Hayward Lakes area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch diameter hail was reported and had 20-30 minutes of melt on them before found.

For the second consecutive day, a line of powerful thunderstorms moved across portions of central and east-central Wisconsin. The worst damage occurred from Wood/Portage counties eastward into the Fox Valley and lakeshore. Trees were uprooted or snapped and several structures were damaged as a result of straight-line winds and six weak, fast-moving tornadoes.||There was also a fatality due to drowning in Keshena (Menominee Co.) from abnormally fast flows on the Wolf River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado damaged several trees (DI 27,28, DOD 2,3) on the south side of Weyauwega. Peak wind estimated at 75 mph.

The tornado continued northeast and destroyed a storage shed (DI 1, DOD 4,5), heavily damaged trees (DI 28, DOD 3,4), ripped shingles and siding off a house (DI 2, DOD 2), and destroyed part of a wooden deck. A power pole also fell onto a car. Peak wind speeds were estimated around 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed in very unstable air ahead of a passing cold front. The worst of the damage from the storms occurred across east-central and parts of northeast Wisconsin. Thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees and some power lines, hail as large as golf balls fell, and a weak tornado touched down on the east side of Green Bay (Brown Co.). Heavy rain led to flooding in parts of east-central Wisconsin as the storms formed an east-west line that slowly moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a wind gust to 61 mph at Marshfield Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure approached the region through the day on Monday, October 21st, producing increasingly strong east to northeast winds. The winds increased through the day, and were especially strong coming off Lake Superior into the western South Shore area. Peak wind speeds topped 70 MPH at some of the bridges and waves caused damage along many of the lake shore areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from Lake Superior was being blown onto US Highway 2 on the west side of Ashland and leading to areas of minor flooding on the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone thunderstorm developed along the Mississippi River during the early evening of August 3rd. A funnel cloud formed with the storm and was sighted by several people at a local business west of Centerville (Trempealeau County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted west of Centerville from a storm that had formed along the Mississippi River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across western Wisconsin during the afternoon of August 5th. These storms dropped large hail across portions of Buffalo and Jackson Counties. The largest reported hail was egg sized near Nelson (Buffalo County), in Alma (Buffalo County) and in Osseo (Trempealeau County). The hail in Osseo damaged around 300 vehicles at a dealership. Trees were blown down in the vicinity of Arcadia (Trempealeau County), Westboro (Taylor County) and Mauston (Juneau County). After the storms, more than 1,000 customers were without power across portions of Clark County and around 2,400 customers lost power in Mauston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Egg sized hail fell near Nelson.

A 60 mph wind gust occurred near Spencer (Clark County) and trees were blown down across portions of Clark, Jackson and Adams Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Friendship.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed in very unstable air ahead of a passing cold front. The worst of the damage from the storms occurred across east-central and parts of northeast Wisconsin. Thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees and some power lines, hail as large as golf balls fell, and a weak tornado touched down on the east side of Green Bay (Brown Co.). Heavy rain led to flooding in parts of east-central Wisconsin as the storms formed an east-west line that slowly moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Rhinelander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed in very unstable air ahead of a passing cold front. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado quickly formed southeast of the city of Green Bay and moved southeast into the town of Bellevue. Three power poles sustained damage and a business sign was heavily damaged in Green Bay. The tornado continued southeast and damaged at least 100 trees along the path (DI 27 and 28, DOD 1-3). A poorly constructed carport lost part of its metal roof at an apartment building and a nearby house lost a couple shingles (DI 2, DOD 2).

Kewaunee Police say the man was working for a company that was contracted to do some work in the area and had been holding an uncharged power line in the performance of his job when lightning struck. He was transported to the hospital and his condition is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of weak low pressure and its cold front brought a line of strong to severe thunderstorms to southern WI during the evening.

Mild temperatures and some rainfall led to snow melt and excessive runoff on a frozen ground. Numerous rivers flooded including flooding in atypical areas due to ice jams. Evacuations were needed in some communities.  The American Red Cross did respond in some cases to aid the flood victims.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams on Spring Creek and the Crawfish River led to significant flooding in the Lodi and Columbus areas, respectively.  Several businesses in the downtown area of Lodi were flooded, and roads were flooded and closed upstream and downstream of Spring Creek.   An ice jam at the highway 16/60 bridge in Columbus caused significant flooding near the river. 40 residents were evacuated from apartments and homes. Highway 16/60 was closed from County Road TT in Dodge County to highway 73 in Columbus.  Additional road closures occurred upstream on the branches of the Crawfish River near the Village of Fall River and Doylestown. Just southwest of Columbus, County Highway K was closed due to flooding from Robbins Creek. Farther north, the Fox River flooded, including Park Lake in Pardeeville, where severe erosion occurred to one property losing trees and soil along its shoreline.  Highway 44 was closed in a couple sections northeast of Pardeeville due to the flooding of the Fox River. In the southwest portion of the county, the Crystal Lake Campground and RV Park was evacuated due to the high levels of Crystal Lake on March 15th, but conditions improved by the next day.|Flooding also occurred in low lying areas across the county which closed a few additional rural roads, including south of Arlington and a small section of highway 188 in the Town of West Point near Lake Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and some rainfall led to snow melt and excessive runoff on a frozen ground. Numerous rivers flooded including flooding in atypical areas due to ice jams. Evacuations were needed in some communities.  The American Red Cross did respond in some cases to aid the flood victims.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were flooded and closed in the Township of Avon due to the flooding of the Sugar River and due to lowland flooding. Portions of other roads were closed into central and northeast Rock Co., more specifically in the townships of Spring Valley, Rock, Plymouth, Fulton, and Johnstown due to lowland flooding and creek flooding. A portion of highway 51 was flooded and closed in Edgerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and some rainfall led to snow melt and excessive runoff on a frozen ground. Numerous rivers flooded including flooding in atypical areas due to ice jams. Evacuations were needed in some communities.  The American Red Cross did respond in some cases to aid the flood victims.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of many roads flooded and closed mainly over the western half of Dane Co. More specifically, flooded roads in the deeper valleys and near creeks and the Sugar River. Large portions of highway 78 was flooded and closed and highway 69 from US 151 to near Paoli. Highway 19 was closed at the intersection with highway KP. Portions of South Point Rd. on the far west side of Madison was flooded and closed. Several streets and adjacent yards in De Forest were flooded by the Yahara River. Voluntary evacuations occurred in De Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Monday, August, 5th, a line of thunderstorms was moving southeast across east central Minnesota ahead of a cold front that was also moving southeast. These storms moved into west central Wisconsin and produced large hail south of Eau Claire.

During the afternoon of Monday, August, 5th, a line of thunderstorms was moving southeast across east central Minnesota ahead of a cold front that was also moving southeast. These storms moved into west central Wisconsin and produced large hail south of Eau Claire.

The Milwaukee County Medical Examiner's Office reported the death of a man due to hypothermia. The 79-year-old man was found dead on a porch and was not dressed for the weather. He suffered from Alzheimer's and had wandered away from his home. Temperatures were in the single digits in the morning and warmed to 20 degrees Fahrenheit in the afternoon.

He suffered from Alzheimer's and had wandered away from his home. Temperatures were in the single digits in the morning and warmed to 20 degrees Fahrenheit in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and some rainfall led to snow melt and excessive runoff on a frozen ground. Numerous rivers flooded including flooding in atypical areas due to ice jams. Evacuations were needed in some communities.  The American Red Cross did respond in some cases to aid the flood victims.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Lake Koshkonong reached major flood stage, cresting at 11.51 feet the early morning of March 25th. This flooding continued into April. Lake Koshkonong merges with Mud Lake and the Rock River on the east side of the lake. There is knee-deep water over Blackhawk Island Rd. and Lamp Rd. is flooded.  Flooding on Oxbow Bend Rd. in the Town of Koshkonong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures, snow melt, and precipitation during the month of March eventually led to minor flooding on the WI River at Portage for the last week of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WI River at Portage reached minor flood stage, cresting at 17.73 feet the early morning of March 28th. In the Blackhawk Park area in the Town of Caledonia, Old River Road is closed with water over it. West Lane Road in the Long Lake area is closed with about 4 feet of water over it in places. Wood Street is affected by floodwaters. In the Town of Dekorra, floodwaters are over portions of Oak Knoll Road and River Oaks Road. Several homes have water in the first floor and there is also flooding of outbuildings and yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and some rainfall led to snow melt and excessive runoff on a frozen ground. Numerous rivers flooded including flooding in atypical areas due to ice jams. Evacuations were needed in some communities.  The American Red Cross did respond in some cases to aid the flood victims.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pecatonica River at Martintown reached major flood stage, cresting at 22.41 feet during the early afternoon of March 16th. Some homes in the Martintown area have floodwaters in their basements and first floors with major damage. Martintown Road and West River Road in Martintown are flooded. The Pecatonica River was flooding upstream to the border with Lafayette County. In Browntown, Hwy 11 is limited to one lane between highway M and Cadiz Spring Rd. Portions of Highway MM and West Indies Road are flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure area brought a prolonged period of light to moderate snow to southern WI from the evening of October 30th through much of October 31st. Due to the relatively mild air temperatures and road temperatures, most major roadways remained wet while secondary roads did have some minor snow accumulation. Overall, 4 to 8 inches of wet snow fell over southern WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and some rainfall led to snow melt and excessive runoff on a frozen ground. Numerous rivers flooded including flooding in atypical areas due to ice jams. Evacuations were needed in some communities.  The American Red Cross did respond in some cases to aid the flood victims.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant ice jam at the Milwaukee River at Waubeka closed the Cigrant Rd. bridge.  Just upstream from the bridge, 20 homes were impacted by flooding and 50 people were evacuated. The American Red Cross gave aid to the evacuees.  Much farther upstream, a lift station in the Village of Newburg was damaged. An Emergency Declaration was issued due to excessive flooding, although the flooding primarily was lowland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheboygan River flooded low lying areas along the river due to several ice jams. The most impacted area was from just east of Kohler downstream to Kiwanis Park in Sheboygan. Portions of Indiana Ave. and New Jersey Ave., as well as other roads were closed due to flooding. At least one vehicle was submerged in the floodwaters on New Jersey Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure brought a swath of moderate snow across south central and east central WI from late evening on October 28th into the early morning hours of October 29th. Snow accumulation of 3 to 4 inches occurred with some slick roads reported. The weight of the snow on trees, that still had their leaves, did cause tree limbs to break and land on power lines. Some power outages occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 3 to 4 inches.

The floodwaters did not recede until the last weekend in March. There was only minor flood damage to the runway and buildings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Branch of the Rock River reached moderate flood stage, cresting at 8.66 feet during the early morning of March 15th in Waupun. Water was flowing around the north end of the city of Waupun Dam, reconnecting with the river. Gateway Dr. near the river was flooded and closed. Various parks along the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system brought a prolonged period of mixed wintry precipitation to north-central Wisconsin. Most of the precipitation fell as snow, but there were some periods of sleet. The snow began during the late afternoon of November 30th and continued into December 1st. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Medford (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across Taylor County. The snow began during the late afternoon of November 30th with most of the snow falling on December 1st. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Medford.

Several county roads were closed throughout the county.

The main highways of 23 & 81 are closed as is Main St.  The Lafayette County fairgrounds is flooded. The Pecatonica River is flooding portions of Calamine upstream from Darlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across portions of north-central Wisconsin along a warm front during the early afternoon hours. The strongest storm produced large hail as it moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in White Lake. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across portions of north-central Wisconsin along a warm front during the early afternoon hours. The strongest storm produced large hail as it moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell just northwest of Mountain. The time of this event is an estimate based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front caused a line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and move through central and north-central Wisconsin during the evening hours. The storms mainly produced wind damage as they moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed some trees on Highway 45 between Eagle River and Conover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across North Dakota during the afternoon of Monday, September 2nd. This line of storms continued to move southeast across Minnesota during the evening. These storms moved into west central Wisconsin but weakened considerably with only a few small limbs blown down, and a wind gust of 59 mph at the Eau Claire Airport.

At the beginning of the storm evolution, a few discrete cells produced a tornado near Elk Mound. A very favorable environment capable of producing supercells and tornadoes was in place across west central Wisconsin. Gradual development of thunderstorms occurred during the early evening with widespread storms stretching from northwestern Iowa, southern Minnesota, into western and northern Wisconsin. A few became severe, but the number of storms probably limited most from becoming very strong. The supercell that produced the strong EF3 tornado was an exception.  It rapidly intensified and went on to produce the first September EF3 tornado in Wisconsin since Ladysmith on 9/2/2002. Also, the last EF3 tornado this late in the season occurred on 9/28/1971.||As the storms evolved during the evening, a broken line developed, with some straight-line wind damage east of the tornado in Chippewa Falls.||The local emergency manager provided preliminary damage estimates. It includes the costs of repairing or replacing the 29 homes that were damaged or destroyed. The 22 single-family homes were valued at $2.68 million, and the seven manufactured homes were valued at $136,500, for a total housing cost of $2.7 million. With repairs to public utilities, the cost rose to $3.0 million in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey determined a tornado began on the south side of Elk Mound, near the intersection of I-94, and South Holly Road. The tornado moved northeast, crossing I-94, and continued into Chippewa County as it crossed 10th Street, south of 560th Avenue. Most of the damage was EF0 in Dunn County where a barn lost its roof, and trees were uprooted or blown down. The path length in Dunn County was 2.3 miles long, with a maximum width of 250 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. idespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were observed with this storm with heavier amounts to around 2 feet along the South Shore of Lake Superior.

A prolonged period of showers and storms during the early morning hours of September 12th created some flash flooding across portions of Grant County. Near Beetown, a mudslide occurred on County Highway U and a township road was closed because of water over it. High water also occurred along State Highway 35 near the intersection of State Highway 133 in Bloomington. Radar estimates indicate between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest of Burton, 4.55 inches of rain fell.

The tornado crossed Highway 29, and move northeast until dissipating east of 50th Street, north of County Road X. ||Based on the NWS Storm Survey, the most significant damage occurred in Wheaton Township, or east of Elk Mound around the intersection of County Road M, and Highway 29. This area saw entire groves of trees leveled, some stripping of bark, and the destruction of several structures. The destruction of a metal building and two nearby homes were consistent with expected damage from winds of EF3 in strength.||The path length in Chippewa County was 4.7 miles, with a maximum width of 725 yards, or nearly a half mile wide. It rapidly intensified and went on to produce the first September EF3 tornado in Wisconsin since Ladysmith on 9/2/2002. Also, the last EF3 tornado this late in the season occurred on 9/28/1971.||As the storms evolved during the evening, a broken line developed, with some straight-line wind damage east of the tornado in Chippewa Falls.||The local emergency manager provided preliminary damage estimates. It includes the costs of repairing or replacing the 29 homes that were damaged or destroyed. The 22 single-family homes were valued at $2.68 million, and the seven manufactured homes were valued at $136,500, for a total housing cost of $2.7 million. With repairs to public utilities, the cost rose to $3.0 million in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree blew down in Chippewa Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and powerful winter storm spread snow across the Northland starting the evening of the 29th and brought a few waves of snow into the afternoon of the 30th. The low then deepened and passed to the south of the region, bringing heavy snowfall and high winds the evening of the 30th into the morning of the 1st. idespread snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were observed with this storm with heavier amounts to around 2 feet along the South Shore of Lake Superior.

Low pressure developed over Wyoming and Montana on the 8th and moved eastward across the Dakotas and into northeastern Iowa on the morning of the 9th, setting up an area of snow to the north of the low that passed across the Northland. Snow began the evening of the 8th and ended for most the afternoon of the 9th, although lake effect snows of Lake Superior lasted into the 10th before ending. Snowfall totals of 6 to 8 inches were common across much of northwestern Wisconsin with a high of just over 10 in northeastern Douglas County that was aided by lake enhancement from Lake Superior.

A strong low pressure area over the northern Great Lakes spread a swath of accumulating snow across southern WI.  Roads became snow covered and slippery with some drifting snow via the brisk winds. There were many vehicle slide-offs and accidents reported on highway 16 in Jefferson and Waukesha Counties during the evening, as well as Interstate 94 east of Madison to Waukesha.  A lesser number of vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred over the remainder of southern WI.

Flooding that began in March along the Mississippi River temporarily ended in early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Mississippi River at La Crosse that began in March, temporarily ended in early May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With water levels still high from the spring snowmelt flooding, another round of flooding occurred on the Mississippi River in late May from runoff after a couple of rainfall events. The flooding continued into June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff after a couple of rain events pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks again in La Crosse. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 13.24 feet on May 25th. The flooding continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from eastern Kansas into southwestern Wisconsin May 8 into May 9. An area of snow along an inverted trough from the low spread across northwest Wisconsin the night of the 8th. With this system arriving overnight, the warm May sun did not play a role in keeping snowfall accumulations down and snow was able to rapidly accumulate. A rain/snow line did set up across northwest Wisconsin, leading to a sharp cutoff in snowfall amounts across the region. The areas that did see snow the night of the 8th had most of it melt during the day on the 9th with most of it being gone by the next morning. Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was seen along the South Shore of Lake Superior with 9.9 inches near Maple and 9.5 inches near Moquah.

A slow moving area of low pressure moved from near Sioux Falls, SD, the morning of the 18th, to near Cedar Rapids, IA, the morning of the 19th. This brought an area of rain and snow to the Northland on the 19th. Unseasonably cold air was able to move in and lead to more snow than anticipated in the Douglas County area where 4 to 7 inches of snowfall were seen with 7.3 inches inches of snow being recorded in the town of Poplar. Otherwise, a rain/snow mix kept amounts closer to 2 inches or less in the Twin Ports area and northwest Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm in a week struck during the Thanksgiving weekend, impacting Wisconsin from late Saturday evening November 30th through Sunday December 1st. The storm developed over the Central Plains early Saturday, then quickly moved east to the Wisconsin/Illinois border on Sunday. Warm air aloft brought a mix of rain, sleet, freezing rain, and snow to the area Saturday afternoon and Saturday evening, transitioning to all snow by Saturday night and continuing into Sunday. A band of 8 to 12 inches fell across northern Wisconsin, with some areas receiving as much as 14 inches of snow. Farther south, snowfall totals were lower, in the 4 to 8 inch range; however, gusty winds caused some downed trees and power outages across the region.  The combination of high bay and lake water levels and strong northeast winds also caused considerable flooding along the bay and lake.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 14.0 inches in Wausaukee (Marinette Co.), 13.5 inches near Mountain (Oconto Co.), 13.4 inches in Florence (Florence Co.), 12.2 inches in Washington Island (Door Co.),  12.1 inches in Crivitz High Falls (Oconto Co.),  11.6 inches in White Lake (Langlade Co.), and 11.3 inches near Argonne (Forest Co.) as well as Rice Reservoir (Lincoln Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across southern Marinette County included 12.1 inches in Crivitz High Falls, 7.6 inches in Peshtigo and 6.5 inches in Marinette. Freezing drizzle and sleet with icing amounts less than a tenth of an inch fell before the snow arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of September 12th. These storms dropped 3 to 4 inches of rain on the region and with already saturated soils, flash flooding occurred across parts of Grant and Crawford Counties. Across the northwest portion of Grant County, nearly every township road was closed across three townships prompting two school districts to cancel classes on the 13th. In Crawford County, State Highway 35 was closed between Prairie du Chien and Ferryville and U.S. Highway 61 near the Wisconsin River was also closed because of debris on the road. In Prairie du Chien, the mayor declared a state of emergency after numerous homes were damaged by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Kickapoo River out of its banks in Gays Mills. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 13.55 feet on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snow storm that produced nearly a foot of snow along the northern part of Polk, Barron, and Rusk Counties, started Nov 29 and lingered into early Sunday afternoon,  Dec 1, with little additional snowfall.||Heaviest measured snowfall reports:|Barron County, near Haugen, 17.0 inches. |Polk County, near Nye, 8.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm started Saturday, November 30th, and slowly moved through the Upper Midwest through Sunday, December 1st. Bands of light snow occurred as this storm system moved across the region, with the heaviest occurring Saturday night, with locally 8 to 12 inches falling across the county by Sunday morning. Nye reported 8 inches snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Ice Jam and high river stages produced flooding in the city of Oconto, called the worst flooding in decades. Water surrounded many homes and some businesses and the city of Oconto declared a flood emergency. Oconto had similar flooding from ice jams in the late 1800s, 1922, and 1986. An estimated $480,000 worth of damage was reported during this event.||The event began the night of December 30th and continued into the next month, ending in the morning on January 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam and high river stages produced flooding in the city of Oconto.  The high water mainly affected Pecor, McDonald, Water, Turner, Ontario, Bridge, First and Second streets.  The mobile home park on Farnsworth Avenue also Flooded. Washington Street by the Oconto County Courthouse also flooded as storm sewers overflowed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As one storm system began to weaken across southern Minnesota during the evening of Sunday, December 30th, a secondary storm developed across the Great Lakes, and provided an additional surge of moisture across the Upper Midwest on Monday. Bands of light snow started across western Wisconsin as early as late Sunday evening, with the main concentration of snow Monday morning, and into Monday afternoon as the system slowly started to move away from the region. The initial very warm air aloft was quickly cooled by Sunday afternoon as the first system started to wrap in the colder air from the west. The highest total was measured near Nye with a report of 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports of 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across Rusk County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm in a week struck during the Thanksgiving weekend, impacting Wisconsin from late Saturday evening November 30th through Sunday December 1st. The storm developed over the Central Plains early Saturday, then quickly moved east to the Wisconsin/Illinois border on Sunday. Warm air aloft brought a mix of rain, sleet, freezing rain, and snow to the area Saturday afternoon and Saturday evening, transitioning to all snow by Saturday night and continuing into Sunday. A band of 8 to 12 inches fell across northern Wisconsin, with some areas receiving as much as 14 inches of snow. Farther south, snowfall totals were lower, in the 4 to 8 inch range; however, gusty winds caused some downed trees and power outages across the region.  The combination of high bay and lake water levels and strong northeast winds also caused considerable flooding along the bay and lake.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 14.0 inches in Wausaukee (Marinette Co.), 13.5 inches near Mountain (Oconto Co.), 13.4 inches in Florence (Florence Co.), 12.2 inches in Washington Island (Door Co.),  12.1 inches in Crivitz High Falls (Oconto Co.),  11.6 inches in White Lake (Langlade Co.), and 11.3 inches near Argonne (Forest Co.) as well as Rice Reservoir (Lincoln Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds and high waters levels on Lake Michigan caused beach erosion along the Manitowoc County coastline. The high waves caused driftwood and other debris to be washed up onto the bike path from Manitowoc to Two Rivers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of high bay water levels and strong northeast winds caused flooding of Red Arrow Park in Marinette. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of September 12th. These storms dropped 3 to 4 inches of rain on the region and with already saturated soils, flash flooding occurred across parts of Grant and Crawford Counties. Across the northwest portion of Grant County, nearly every township road was closed across three townships prompting two school districts to cancel classes on the 13th. In Crawford County, State Highway 35 was closed between Prairie du Chien and Ferryville and U.S. Highway 61 near the Wisconsin River was also closed because of debris on the road. In Prairie du Chien, the mayor declared a state of emergency after numerous homes were damaged by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Trempealeau River out of its banks in Arcadia. The river crested less than a half foot above the flood stage at 8.31 feet on the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure area over the northern Great Lakes spread a swath of accumulating snow across southern WI.  Roads became snow covered and slippery with some drifting snow via the brisk winds. There were many vehicle slide-offs and accidents reported on highway 16 in Jefferson and Waukesha Counties during the evening, as well as Interstate 94 east of Madison to Waukesha.  A lesser number of vehicle slide-offs and accidents occurred over the remainder of southern WI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 1 to 3 inches. Many vehicle slide-offs and accidents on highway 16 and I-94.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of December ended with a storm system dropping heavy snow on portions of western Wisconsin on the 30th. One person was killed the following day in a snow plowing  accident involving two vehicles near Lublin (Taylor County). Snowfall amounts were generally in the 6 to 8 inch range, although a pocket of heavier snow produced totals around 10 inches across the northern portions of Clark County. The highest reported totals were 9.5 inches near Owen (Clark County) and 10.5 inches in Loyal (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person died after a snow plowing accident involving two vehicles near Lublin. The person killed was using a pickup to push snow across a road and pulled out in front of the second vehicle. COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 7 to 8 inches of snow fell across Taylor County. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Rib Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms that went through the Twin Cities late Monday afternoon, and early evening, moved across portions of Barron and Rusk Counties and produced straight-line wind damage. The most significant occurred north of Bruce where numerous trees were uprooted or blown down. Isolated wind damage occurred early near Chippewa Falls where a few storm developed near Eau Claire and moved northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey indicated wind damage occurred north of Bruce, and tracked eastward for several miles. The wind downed numerous trees, damaged a barn and outbuilding northwest of Ladysmith. Winds were estimated up to 75 mph as most of the barn roof was damaged or lifted off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across southwest Wisconsin during the early morning hours of September 19th. These storms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain that caused water to go over roads in and near Lancaster, Beetown and Ellenboro (Grant County). Water also covered some roads in the vicinity of Ontario (Vernon County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain across Grant County. This rain fell on already saturated soils and created some flash flooding with water over roads in and near Lancaster and Beetown, including U.S. Highway 61 and State Highway 81. State Highway 81 was also closed near Ellenboro because of flooding along the Platte River. In Lancaster, flood waters impacted several city streets and parks.
