EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of December ended with a storm system dropping heavy snow on portions of western Wisconsin on the 30th. One person was killed the following day in a snow plowing  accident involving two vehicles near Lublin (Taylor County). Snowfall amounts were generally in the 6 to 8 inch range, although a pocket of heavier snow produced totals around 10 inches across the northern portions of Clark County. The highest reported totals were 9.5 inches near Owen (Clark County) and 10.5 inches in Loyal (Clark County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 6 inches of snow fell across Buffalo County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms moved across southwest Wisconsin during the afternoon and evening of September 12th. These storms dropped 3 to 4 inches of rain on the region and with already saturated soils, flash flooding occurred across parts of Grant and Crawford Counties. Across the northwest portion of Grant County, nearly every township road was closed across three townships prompting two school districts to cancel classes on the 13th. In Crawford County, State Highway 35 was closed between Prairie du Chien and Ferryville and U.S. Highway 61 near the Wisconsin River was also closed because of debris on the road. In Prairie du Chien, the mayor declared a state of emergency after numerous homes were damaged by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flash flooding to occur near Potosi and Dickeyville. Roads were closed in and near Potosi. Near Dickeyville, an estimated five feet of water from McAdam Branch Creek was flowing over a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of severe storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the evening of September 24th. These storms produced a tornado in Clark County that started southwest of Greenwood and ended west of Loyal. The tornado damaged several farm buildings, silos and trees along its path. High winds from the storms blew down trees from Alma and Modena (Buffalo County), east through Eleva and Osseo (Trempealeau County) to near Humbird (Clark County). The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Modena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of severe storms rolled across western Wisconsin during the evening of September 24th. These storms produced a tornado in Clark County that started southwest of Greenwood and ended west of Loyal. The tornado damaged several farm buildings, silos and trees along its path. High winds from the storms blew down trees from Alma and Modena (Buffalo County), east through Eleva and Osseo (Trempealeau County) to near Humbird (Clark County). The Governor of Wisconsin declared a state of emergency for Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down north of Humbird.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front brought a couple rounds of accumulating snow to much of southern WI. The 1st round affected the morning commute and resulted in some vehicle slide-offs and accidents, including one indirect death from a vehicle accident. The 2nd round of snow was in the afternoon and evening and was not as impactful. Overall, 3 to 5 inches of snow accumulation occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 3 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first winter storm of the season moved across the region in the busy pre-Thanksgiving travel period. The storm dumped a swath of heavy snow over portions of north-central Wisconsin with accumulations generally in the 4 to 6 inch range. In addition to the snow, winds gusts of 30 to 40 mph created drifting snow which further hampered travel across the region. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in Medford (Taylor County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across Taylor County. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in Medford. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph created drifting snow as the storm was departing the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system brought a prolonged period of mixed wintry precipitation to north-central Wisconsin. Most of the precipitation fell as snow, but there were some periods of sleet. The snow began during the late afternoon of November 30th and continued into December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that snow fell across Taylor County during the late afternoon and evening of November 30th with most of the snow falling on December 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system developed over the central Plains and moved northeast to Lake Michigan, bringing 10 to 14 inches of heavy snow, and occasionally sleet, to northern and portions of central Wisconsin. The wet, heavy snow accumulating on trees and power lines, along with strong winds, caused widespread power outages across much of northern Wisconsin with over 40,000 residences impacted.  The combination of strong winds and high water levels caused flooding along the shoreline of the Bay of Green Bay and Lake Michigan.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 14.8 inches near Land O' Lakes (Vilas Co.), 14.1 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 13.5 inches in Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), 12.7 inches in Phelps (Vilas Co.), 11.6 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), 11.0 inches west of Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 10.8 inches in Minoqua (Oneida Co.), and 10.0 inches in Summit Lake (Langlade Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green Bay marina experienced two rounds of flooding. Minor flooding was noted between 4 am and 7 am and with a secondary round of flooding occurring during the early afternoon. The high waters at the Green Bay marina surrounded the nearby Coast Guard Station.

The combination of strong winds and high water levels caused flooding along the shoreline of the Bay of Green Bay and Lake Michigan.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 14.8 inches near Land O' Lakes (Vilas Co.), 14.1 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 13.5 inches in Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), 12.7 inches in Phelps (Vilas Co.), 11.6 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), 11.0 inches west of Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 10.8 inches in Minoqua (Oneida Co.), and 10.0 inches in Summit Lake (Langlade Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High bay water levels and strong easterly winds caused flooding along the Bay of Green Bay. Water inundated portions of Highway Y from Oconto to the Town of Little River, resulting in the road closure of sections of Highway Y and several city streets in Oconto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, and fairly slow moving storm system first developed Friday afternoon November 29th with an area of light snow from the initial surge of warmer air across southern Minnesota. This area of light snow moved across eastern Minnesota, and adjacent western Wisconsin Friday night, with generally one to three inches of snow falling across the region. By Saturday morning, this initial wave of light snow moved north into northeastern Minnesota. A secondary band of precipitation, with some sleet reported with this second band, moved from northern Iowa, across southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin during the morning. This band produced mainly one inch of snow, with some sleet at times. ||During the afternoon, the main storm system moved out into the plains of Nebraska, with an enhanced area of precipitation developing across eastern Minnesota, and western Wisconsin Saturday afternoon, November 30th. Some areas of west central Wisconsin had up to 6 inches of snow by late Saturday afternoon, and into the early morning hours of Sunday, December 1st.||The strongest part of the storm system moved across Iowa Saturday evening, and into southern Wisconsin Sunday morning December 1st. The continued strong surge of moisture wrapped around the area of low pressure in Iowa, with the best area of light to moderate snow falling across west central Wisconsin, and over parts of east central and central Minnesota through early Sunday morning.||By late Sunday morning, most of the measurable snowfall had moved off to the Great Lakes region with some flurries and light snow lingering in western Wisconsin Sunday afternoon.||Although reports in west central Wisconsin were few, counties along the border did receive between 11 and 15 inches of snow.   The heaviest snowfall that was measured occurred near Haugen with a report of 17 inches throughout the three days, including Dec 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow fell across Rusk County, with locally 6 to 8 inches falling by Saturday evening. However, the storm continued into December 1st and produced a few more inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 59 mph in Marquette near Puckaway Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Bayshore Road was often under water due to high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated lake levels on Lake Michigan caused some isolated flooded areas near the lake on an ongoing basis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around the Kewaunee Marina were often under water due to high lake levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anticipated winter storm brought heavy snow across the Upper Midwest. It started late Tuesday afternoon, and quickly exited the region by late Wednesday morning. ||The winter storm was a typical Colorado Low that developed in the central Rockies Monday night, moved out into the plains of Colorado, and tracked east, then northeast across Kansas, northern Missouri, southeast Iowa, and into southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning. The main forcing with this system was at mid-levels and it was also quite fast. The strongest forcing that contributed  to this system was late Tuesday evening, and lasted around 6 hours. Therefore, snowfall amounts were limited to locally 6 to 9 inches where the best forcing and lift occurred as the surface low tracked across eastern Iowa, and into southern/eastern Wisconsin.   Wind speeds were generally 15-25 mph, with drifting snow in open areas.||In west central Wisconsin, the highest totals were 9 to 11 inches, specifically:|Rusk County, near Glen Flora, 11.0 inches. |Dunn County, near Menomonie, 10.2 inches. |Chippewa County, near Bloomer, 9.6 inches.  |Chippewa County, near Chippewa Falls, 9.6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers measured 4 to 8 inches of snow before the snow tapered off Wednesday morning. The heaviest occurred late Tuesday evening, and into the early morning of Wednesday, November 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anticipated winter storm brought heavy snow across the Upper Midwest. It started late Tuesday afternoon, and quickly exited the region by late Wednesday morning. ||The winter storm was a typical Colorado Low that developed in the central Rockies Monday night, moved out into the plains of Colorado, and tracked east, then northeast across Kansas, northern Missouri, southeast Iowa, and into southern Wisconsin by Wednesday morning. The main forcing with this system was at mid-levels and it was also quite fast. The strongest forcing that contributed  to this system was late Tuesday evening, and lasted around 6 hours. Therefore, snowfall amounts were limited to locally 6 to 9 inches where the best forcing and lift occurred as the surface low tracked across eastern Iowa, and into southern/eastern Wisconsin.   Wind speeds were generally 15-25 mph, with drifting snow in open areas.||In west central Wisconsin, the highest totals were 9 to 11 inches, specifically:|Rusk County, near Glen Flora, 11.0 inches. |Dunn County, near Menomonie, 10.2 inches. |Chippewa County, near Bloomer, 9.6 inches.  |Chippewa County, near Chippewa Falls, 9.6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observers measured 6 to 8 inches of snow before the snow tapered off Wednesday morning. The heaviest occurred late Tuesday evening, and into the early morning of Wednesday, November 27th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous branches and trees reported down between Mineral Point and Arena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Afton continued to flood into the first week of November after the heavy rains of early October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Afton continued to flood into the first week of November after cresting in mid October. Floodwaters affect the property of a home on South Christian Road on the east side of the river in the Afton area. There is minor erosional flood damage to lowland and agricultural land in the Afton and Janesville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Texas Panhandle region northeastward to southern Wisconsin from the 26th into the morning of the 27th. Heavy snowfall spread across northwest Wisconsin starting the afternoon and evening of the 26th before tapering off the afternoon of the 27th. Widespread amounts of 6 to 10 inches fell in this area with higher amounts to around a foot in northern Iron County which saw lake effect snow on the back side of the low that contributed to the higher amounts.

High pressure over the Northern Plains and northern High Plains led to on-shore winds off Lake Superior into Iron County. With the lake remaining ice free, heavy lake effect snow bands set up over the northern portion of the county and led to a prolonged period of snowfall. When snow ended after 48 hours, 7 to 16 inches of snow had fallen in the northern half of the county.

A strengthening low pressure system developed over the central Plains and moved northeast to Lake Michigan, bringing 10 to 14 inches of heavy snow, and occasionally sleet, to northern and portions of central Wisconsin. The wet, heavy snow accumulating on trees and power lines, along with strong winds, caused widespread power outages across much of northern Wisconsin with over 40,000 residences impacted.  The combination of strong winds and high water levels caused flooding along the shoreline of the Bay of Green Bay and Lake Michigan.||Some of the highest snowfall totals included 14.8 inches near Land O' Lakes (Vilas Co.), 14.1 inches in Eagle River (Vilas Co.), 13.5 inches in Hazelhurst (Oneida Co.), 12.7 inches in Phelps (Vilas Co.), 11.6 inches in Rhinelander (Oneida Co.), 11.0 inches west of Merrill (Lincoln Co.), 10.8 inches in Minoqua (Oneida Co.), and 10.0 inches in Summit Lake (Langlade Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals in Vilas County included 14.8 inches near Land O' Lakes, 14.1 inches in Eagle River, and 12.7 inches in Phelps.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front brought a couple rounds of accumulating snow to much of southern WI. The 1st round affected the morning commute and resulted in some vehicle slide-offs and accidents, including one indirect death from a vehicle accident. The 2nd round of snow was in the afternoon and evening and was not as impactful. Overall, 3 to 5 inches of snow accumulation occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of weakening thunderstorms moved from the Dakotas the morning of September 5th ahead of an area of low pressure. These storms moved across north-central Minnesota during the morning hours and generally exhibited a downward trend as the moved east-southeastward. In the afternoon, a second round of storms formed with the aid of daytime heating in northwest Wisconsin. The largest reports received were of quarter-sized hail as the storms mainly moved over forested areas.

Several strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along the leading edge of a mesoscale convective system that formed earlier in the day across the Dakotas, and moved eastward across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Most of the storms were non-severe with locally heavy rainfall reported. Storms that moved across eastern Minnesota during the afternoon, intensified in west central Wisconsin, west of Rusk County. These storms produced a nearly 8 mile tornado track from near Fireside Lake, northeast to just east of the Flambeau River. Numerous trees were uprooted along this track with several of the trees landing on cabins. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey indicated that an EF0 tornado touched down on the west side of Fireside Lake, on Mud Lake Road. This tornado tracked approximately 7.6 miles northeast, before lifting on the east side of the Flambeau River, west of Sheep Camp Road. The tornado was on the ground for 14 minutes with a path width of 100 yards, and a maximum wind speed of 85 mph. Numerous trees were blown down or uprooted along the path. A few trees blew down on lake cabins causing considerable damage. No injuries occurred with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved east along a stalled out frontal boundary through central and east-central Wisconsin during the morning and early afternoon hours. 1 to 3 inches of rain fell during the morning and afternoon hours. There were multiple reports of flash flooding with streets flooded, businesses and residents evacuated, and some water rescues from stranded motorists. Northeast winds backed up the Fox River and exacerbated the flooding as rainfall could not drain into area rivers efficiently.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street closed due to flash flooding and vehicles stranded in Darboy. A culvert was washed out in the village of Sherwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures caused rapid snow melt that combined with heavy rainfall to produce flooding and rapid flows on the Menominee River near and downstream from McAllister (Marinette Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall and snow melt led to minor flooding and rapid flows on the Menominee River near and downstream from McAllister. Shortly before the flooding subsided, a fishing boat capsized in Marinette causing two men to fall into the river. One was rescued shortly thereafter and was treated and released from the hospital. The other drowned and his body was discovered two days later near Oconto in the waters of Green Bay, more than 15 miles from Marinette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over Colorado and slowly tracked into western Iowa over a two-day period. Repeated bands of snow and mixed precipitation tracked through southern Wisconsin from Wednesday, April 10 through Thursday, April 11, 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a fatal two-car crash Wednesday, April 10th in the afternoon near the intersection of Hwy CS and Wilson Road in the town of Dekorra. Law enforcement said roadway conditions appeard to have been a factor in the crash, and weather reports showed it was snowing at the time of the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tens of thousands of trees were broken, uprooted or downed across several counties, with Polk and Barron Counties being hit the hardest. Many outbuildings were destroyed by wind, along with roofs off barns and sheds. The vast majority of damage was due to a derecho that started over east central Minnesota, and progressed southeast across Wisconsin, and into lower parts of Michigan. The path length was nearly 490 miles long. Three tornadoes also produced damage, but areal extent of the tornadoes paled in comparison to the damage caused by the downbursts.||During the late morning, and early afternoon of Friday, July 19th, a surface low moved from northeast South Dakota, eastward across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by the evening. There was a tight thermal gradient along the surface low/warm front across central Minnesota. During the afternoon, dew points rose to near 80 in the warm sector, with 50s/60s north of the front. This contrast produced MLCAPEs of near 5000 J/kg south of the front boundary. Effective wind shear parameters were very strong with values of 50-70 knots across central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. This environment allowed for supercells to form which congealed into a bow echo that raced across east central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Very large hail, damaging straight-line winds, and a few tornadoes occurred with this large bow echo. ||Based on a collection of video, pictures, and collaboration of local law enforcement offices, and emergency managers, there were thousands of trees uprooted or blown down in a swath that went from Polk County, southeast across Barron, Dunn, Chippewa and parts of Rusk Counties. Embedded in the straight-line wind damage, a few tornadoes occurred. ||The first tornado occurred east of Balsam Lake, and tracked east to north of Turtle Lake where it crossed into Barron County. The tornado continued into Barron County before dissipating east of Turtle Lake. This tornado was rated EF1 that was on the ground for nearly 14 miles. A weaker tornado occurred east of Fox Creek and was only on the ground for a minute with a path length of around 1 mile. ||The county of Chippewa indicated that most of the damage was from the Village of New Auburn in the northwest to Otter Lake in the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intense bow echo entered Polk County in the far northwest corner. It moved southeast and caused significant damage to trees and power lines along its path. A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 73 knots in Cushing. This individual also measured sustained winds of nearly 70 mph for 5 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were thousands of trees broken, blown down or uprooted in a swath that was about eight miles wide, with the north/south extent from northwest of Comstock to  Turtle Lake. The swath extended to the east-southeast to the southwest side of the city of Barron.  Many trees landed on sheds, vehicles and outbuildings. Many outbuildings were destroyed by wind, along with roofs off barns and sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding from March continued into April although most rivers were declining during April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rock River at Fort Atkinson crested at moderate flood stage in late March and  continued to flood into the 1st week of April. The river walkway in Fort Atkinson is flooded and closed.  On the west side of Fort Atkinson, the southern portion of Rock River Park is flooded and Sinissippi Dr. is closed.  On the east side of Fort Atkinson, Bark River Rd. from Kutz Road to Rock River Rd. is flooded and closed. Rock River Rd. from highway 106 to the Fort Atkinson city limits is flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant snow melt over northern WI and some rainfall led to major flood stage on the WI River at Portage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WI River at Portage reached major flood stage, cresting at 20.08 feet the late evening of April 21st.  Much of the Blackhawk Park and Long Lake areas and its numerous roads and some homes are flooded. Some evacuations occurred in these areas. In the city of Portage, portions of  W. Conant St. and Summit St. are flooded including the boat launch. Water rises through the ground and floods portions of Sunset and Franklin Streets. The Fire Department built a berm on Summit  St. which prevented the expansion of flooding into more residential areas. Basement flooding was occurring |for homes near the floodwaters. Upstream from Portage, Levee Rd. is closed from highway 33 just south of Portage to Schepp Rd. in Sauk Co. Residents living on Levee Rd. were evacuated due to some erosion of the Caledonia levee which protects the area from substantial flooding. Portions of River Shores Rd. was also flooded on the east side of the river. Downstream from Portage in the Town of Dekorra, around a dozen homes are flooded on highway V. Portions of highway V, River Oaks Rd., and Oak Knoll Dr. are flooded. Across the river and slightly upstream from the Town of Dekorra, a portion of Thunderbird Rd. is flooded.
